1.1: The LORD spoke to Moses from the tent of meeting in the wilderness of Sinai — on the first of the second month, in the second year after the exodus — saying,
1.2: Take a full count of all the Israelite community — by clans, by ancestral households — recording by name every male, one by one.
1.3: Every man twenty years and older who is fit for military service — you and Aaron shall muster them into their fighting companies.
1.4: One man from every tribe shall stand beside you — each the recognized head of his family's line.
1.5: These are the men appointed to stand with you: from Reuben, Elizur son of Shedeur;
1.6: from Simeon, Shelumiel son of Zurishaddai;
1.7: from Judah, Nahshon son of Amminadab;
1.8: from Issachar, Nethanel son of Zuar;
1.9: from Zebulun, Eliab son of Helon;
1.10: from Joseph's line: from Ephraim, Elishama son of Ammihud; from Manasseh, Gamaliel son of Pedahzur;
1.11: from Benjamin, Abidan son of Gideoni;
1.12: from Dan, Ahiezer son of Ammishaddai;
1.13: from Asher, Pagiel son of Ochran;
1.14: from Gad, Eliasaph son of Deuel;
1.15: from Naphtali, Ahira son of Enan.
1.16: These were Israel's appointed spokesmen — the tribal heads, each the commanding figure of his people's clan.
1.17: Moses and Aaron gathered the men who had been named,
1.18: and convened the full community on the first of the second month. Each person traced his lineage by clan and ancestral household; every male twenty and older was registered individually.
1.19: Moses conducted the census in the wilderness of Sinai, exactly as the LORD had commanded.
1.20: Reuben, firstborn of Israel: men registered by clan and ancestral household, every male twenty and older fit for battle —
1.21: the muster of Reuben: forty-six thousand five hundred.
1.22: Simeon: registered by clan and household, every male twenty and older fit for battle —
1.23: the muster of Simeon: fifty-nine thousand three hundred.
1.24: Gad: registered by clan and household, every male twenty and older fit for battle —
1.25: the muster of Gad: forty-five thousand six hundred and fifty.
1.26: Judah: registered by clan and household, every male twenty and older fit for battle —
1.27: the muster of Judah: seventy-four thousand six hundred — the largest of the eastern camp.
1.28: Issachar: registered by clan and household, every male twenty and older fit for battle —
1.29: the muster of Issachar: fifty-four thousand four hundred.
1.30: Zebulun: registered by clan and household, every male twenty and older fit for battle —
1.31: the muster of Zebulun: fifty-seven thousand four hundred.
1.32: Ephraim, from the house of Joseph: registered by clan and household, every male twenty and older fit for battle —
1.33: the muster of Ephraim: forty thousand five hundred.
1.34: Manasseh, also from the house of Joseph: registered by clan and household, every male twenty and older fit for battle —
1.35: the muster of Manasseh: thirty-two thousand two hundred.
1.36: Benjamin: registered by clan and household, every male twenty and older fit for battle —
1.37: the muster of Benjamin: thirty-five thousand four hundred.
1.38: Dan: registered by clan and household, every male twenty and older fit for battle —
1.39: the muster of Dan: sixty-two thousand seven hundred.
1.40: Asher: registered by clan and household, every male twenty and older fit for battle —
1.41: the muster of Asher: forty-one thousand five hundred.
1.42: Naphtali: registered by clan and household, every male twenty and older fit for battle —
1.43: the muster of Naphtali: fifty-three thousand four hundred.
1.44: These were counted by Moses, Aaron, and the twelve tribal chieftains — one per ancestral house — each responsible for his own people's muster.
1.45: All Israelites twenty and older who were fit for battle, registered by ancestral households —
1.46: came to six hundred three thousand five hundred and fifty men — Israel's fighting force at Sinai.
1.47: The Levites alone were not enrolled in this military census — they stood apart by God's design.
1.48: For the LORD had instructed Moses:
1.49: Do not number Levi or include them in the count of Israel's fighting men.
1.50: Assign the Levites instead to the tabernacle of the covenant: they will bear it, tend all its furnishings, perform its service, and encamp as its living guard on every side.
1.51: When the camp moves, Levites dismantle and carry the tabernacle; when the camp halts, they erect it again. Any outsider who approaches it dies.
1.52: Each Israelite camps by his own unit, under the battle standard of his tribe.
1.53: The Levites form a sacred perimeter around the tabernacle of the covenant — standing between the congregation and the holiness that would consume anyone who trespassed it. They bear responsibility for the tabernacle.
1.54: The Israelites carried out every command the LORD had given Moses — completely and without exception.

2.1: The LORD gave Moses and Aaron this instruction:
2.2: Every Israelite shall camp under his tribal banner, his family's ensign identifying his place — each unit encircling the tent of meeting from a respectful distance.
2.3: On the east — the side of the sunrise — Judah's battle standard leads the first camp, with Nahshon son of Amminadab as its commander.
2.4: Judah's muster: seventy-four thousand six hundred.
2.5: Encamped alongside Judah: Issachar, under Nethanel son of Zuar.
2.6: Issachar's muster: fifty-four thousand four hundred.
2.7: And Zebulun alongside them, under Eliab son of Helon.
2.8: Zebulun's muster: fifty-seven thousand four hundred.
2.9: The eastern camp of Judah — Judah, Issachar, and Zebulun together — numbered one hundred eighty-six thousand four hundred. They lead the march.
2.10: On the south: Reuben's battle standard commands the second camp, with Elizur son of Shedeur as its chief.
2.11: Reuben's muster: forty-six thousand five hundred.
2.12: Alongside Reuben: Simeon, under Shelumiel son of Zurishaddai.
2.13: Simeon's muster: fifty-nine thousand three hundred.
2.14: And Gad alongside them, under Eliasaph son of Reuel.
2.15: Gad's muster: forty-five thousand six hundred and fifty.
2.16: The southern camp of Reuben — Reuben, Simeon, and Gad — numbered one hundred fifty-one thousand four hundred and fifty. They march second.
2.17: In the center of the procession comes the tent of meeting, carried by the Levites. They march at Israel's heart, surrounded by the tribal camps, each unit moving in its ordained place.
2.18: On the west: Ephraim's standard leads the third camp, with Elishama son of Ammihud as its chief.
2.19: Ephraim's muster: forty thousand five hundred.
2.20: Alongside Ephraim: Manasseh, the brother tribe, under Gamaliel son of Pedahzur.
2.21: Manasseh's muster: thirty-two thousand two hundred.
2.22: And Benjamin alongside them, under Abidan son of Gideoni.
2.23: Benjamin's muster: thirty-five thousand four hundred.
2.24: The western camp of Ephraim — Ephraim, Manasseh, and Benjamin — numbered one hundred eight thousand one hundred. They march third.
2.25: On the north: Dan's standard commands the rear camp, with Ahiezer son of Ammishaddai as its chief.
2.26: Dan's muster: sixty-two thousand seven hundred.
2.27: Alongside Dan: Asher, under Pagiel son of Ochran.
2.28: Asher's muster: forty-one thousand five hundred.
2.29: And Naphtali alongside them, under Ahira son of Enan.
2.30: Naphtali's muster: fifty-three thousand four hundred.
2.31: The northern camp of Dan — Dan, Asher, and Naphtali — numbered one hundred fifty-seven thousand six hundred. They march as the rear guard, each under their standards.
2.32: All told, the Israelites registered by ancestral household — every fighting man across all four encampments — came to six hundred three thousand five hundred and fifty.
2.33: The Levites remained uncounted in this census — just as the LORD had commanded Moses.
2.34: Israel obeyed entirely: they camped and marched as the LORD had ordered — each man under his clan's standard, each unit in its appointed place.

3.1: This is the family record of Aaron and Moses from the time the LORD spoke to Moses at Sinai.
3.2: Aaron's sons were Nadab — the firstborn — then Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar.
3.3: These were Aaron's sons — anointed and installed in their priestly office to serve before God.
3.4: Nadab and Abihu died in the LORD's presence — struck down for offering unauthorized fire in the Sinai wilderness, leaving no sons. After them, Eleazar and Ithamar carried out the priestly ministry under their father Aaron's oversight.
3.6: Bring the Levites forward and place them in service to Aaron the priest — assigned to assist him.
3.7: They will carry out Aaron's responsibilities and the congregation's obligations before the tent of meeting — managing the entire service of the dwelling.
3.8: They will care for every piece of equipment in the tent of meeting and perform the Israelites' sacred service on their behalf.
3.9: The Levites are given entirely to Aaron and his sons — a dedicated gift from the whole people of Israel.
3.10: Commission Aaron and his sons to guard the priesthood. Any outsider who intrudes into that sacred office is to be executed.
3.12: I myself have taken the Levites from Israel to stand in place of every firstborn son — the one who first opens the womb. They are mine.
3.13: Every firstborn belongs to me — I claimed them all when I struck down Egypt's firstborn. From that day, every firstborn of Israel, man and beast, is consecrated to me. I am the LORD.
3.14: The LORD spoke again to Moses in the Sinai wilderness:
3.15: Enroll the Levites by their ancestral households and clans — counting every male from a month old and older.
3.16: Moses obeyed, numbering them exactly as the LORD commanded.
3.17: Levi's sons were three: Gershon, Kohath, and Merari.
3.18: Gershon's sons were Libni and Shimei — the two clans of Gershon.
3.19: Kohath's sons were Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel — four clans.
3.20: Merari's sons were Mahli and Mushi. These, then, are the Levite clans, each organized within its ancestral house.
3.21: The Gershonite branch divided into the Libni clan and the Shimei clan.
3.22: The Gershonite males — from one month and older — numbered seven thousand five hundred.
3.23: The Gershonites camp on the western side — behind the tabernacle.
3.24: Their clan chief is Eliasaph son of Lael.
3.25: The Gershonites are entrusted with the outer shell of the tabernacle: the fabric dwelling itself, the tent covering that overlays it, and the screen at its entrance —
3.26: the courtyard hangings, the entrance screen, all the ropes — everything related to the outer fabric structure of the complex.
3.27: The Kohathite branch divided into four clans: Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel.
3.28: The Kohathite males — from one month and older — numbered eight thousand six hundred; they bear the charge of the sanctuary itself.
3.29: The Kohathites camp on the southern side.
3.30: Their clan chief is Elizaphan son of Uzziel.
3.31: The Kohathites guard the most sacred objects: the ark, the table of the Presence, the lampstand, the altars, the sacred vessels — the very heart of Israel's worship.
3.32: Eleazar, Aaron's son and a priest himself, stands as commander over all the Levite chiefs — the overseer of the entire sanctuary guard.
3.33: The Merarite branch divided into two clans: Mahli and Mushi.
3.34: The Merarite males — from one month and older — numbered six thousand two hundred.
3.35: Their clan chief is Zuriel son of Abihail; the Merarites camp on the northern side.
3.36: The Merarites bear the structural skeleton of the tabernacle: the wooden frames, the crossbars, the upright pillars, the socket bases — every piece of the rigid superstructure.
3.37: They also handle the courtyard pillars with their bases, stakes, and ropes.
3.38: Moses, Aaron, and Aaron's sons camped on the east — directly before the tent of meeting, facing the sunrise. They held the foremost charge: guarding the sanctuary on behalf of all Israel. Any outsider who approached was to die.
3.39: The full Levite census — conducted by Moses and Aaron under the LORD's command, every male from a month and older across all the clans — came to twenty-two thousand.
3.40: The LORD told Moses: 'Now count every firstborn male in Israel from a month old and older — by name.'
3.41: Take the Levites as mine — I am the LORD — as substitutes for every firstborn son in Israel, and their livestock as substitutes for every firstborn animal in Israel.
3.42: Moses obeyed and counted every firstborn in Israel.
3.43: The total: twenty-two thousand two hundred and seventy-three firstborn males, one month and older.
3.45: Take the Levites in exchange for all of Israel's firstborn — and their animals in exchange for Israel's firstborn animals. The Levites belong to me. I am the LORD.
3.46: But there are two hundred and seventy-three firstborn sons with no Levite counterpart to substitute for them —
3.47: for each of them you shall collect five shekels, weighed by the sanctuary standard of twenty gerahs per shekel.
3.48: The redemption silver goes to Aaron and his sons.
3.49: Moses collected the redemption payment for those two hundred and seventy-three who had no Levite substitute.
3.50: The total collected from Israel's firstborn came to one thousand three hundred and sixty-five shekels, weighed by the sanctuary standard.
3.51: Moses handed the silver to Aaron and his sons — fulfilling the word of the LORD exactly as commanded.

4.1: The LORD said to Moses and Aaron:
4.2: Count the Kohathites within Levi — by clans and ancestral households —
4.3: specifically those between thirty and fifty years old who are in the prime of service for the tent of meeting.
4.4: The Kohathites have the most demanding assignment: the most holy objects themselves.
4.5: When the camp is about to move, Aaron and his sons enter first: they take down the inner curtain and drape it over the ark of the covenant.
4.6: Over the curtain they lay a durable hide, then a cloth of solid blue, and they slide in the carrying poles — ready for transport.
4.7: The table of the Presence is draped in blue cloth, with all its vessels arranged in place — the plates, saucers, bowls, and pitchers for the drink offering, and the display bread still set upon it.
4.8: A scarlet overlay goes on top, then fine leather, then in go the poles.
4.9: The lampstand and all its components — lamps, snuffers, trays, the oil flasks — are wrapped in blue cloth,
4.10: then placed inside a leather cover and laid on the carrying frame.
4.11: The golden incense altar gets its own blue cloth covering, then a leather wrap, and its poles.
4.12: Every other vessel of sanctuary service is wrapped in blue, covered in leather, and loaded onto the carrying frame.
4.13: The bronze altar is cleared of its ashes and draped in a purple covering.
4.14: All the altar tools — the fire pans, flesh hooks, shovels, basins — are laid upon it, a leather cover goes over everything, and the poles go in.
4.15: Only after Aaron and his sons have completed every covering may the Kohathites approach to lift their loads. The holy objects must not be touched — to touch them is to die. These are the sacred things the Kohathites carry.
4.16: Eleazar the priest holds personal oversight of the consumables: the lamp oil, the incense blend, the daily grain offering, the anointing oil — and through them, supervisory responsibility for the entire tabernacle complex.
4.17: The LORD said to Moses and Aaron:
4.18: Take care that the Kohathite branch of Levi is not destroyed —
4.19: deal with them correctly so they stay alive when they approach those most holy objects: Aaron and his sons must go in first and assign each Kohathite to his specific object and specific duty.
4.20: The Kohathites must not enter and catch even a glance of the holy things while uncovered. A single look means death.
4.22: Count the Gershonites too — by ancestral household and clan.
4.23: Those between thirty and fifty who are qualified for the service — count them all.
4.24: The Gershonites handle the fabric work — carrying and setting up the coverings and curtains.
4.25: They carry the tabernacle curtains, the tent covering, the outer leather layer, the entrance screen —
4.26: the courtyard hangings, the entrance screen, all the ropes — everything fabric that encloses the sacred compound.
4.27: Every aspect of the Gershonite work — each load, each duty — is performed under Aaron and his sons' orders. You are to assign them their specific responsibilities.
4.28: This is the Gershonite mandate in the tent of meeting — supervised by Ithamar, Aaron's son.
4.29: Count the Merarites the same way — by clans and ancestral households.
4.30: Those between thirty and fifty who qualify for this work — count them all.
4.31: The Merarites carry the structural skeleton: the frames, crossbars, pillars, and socket-bases of the tabernacle —
4.32: the courtyard pillars with their bases, stakes, and ropes. Each man is assigned by name to his specific piece of equipment.
4.33: This is the Merarite assignment in the tent of meeting — also supervised by Ithamar, Aaron's son.
4.34: Moses, Aaron, and the tribal leaders conducted the Kohathite count by clans and households —
4.35: every Kohathite man between thirty and fifty fit for service.
4.36: The Kohathite muster came to two thousand seven hundred and fifty.
4.37: These were the registered Kohathite servants of the tent of meeting — numbered by Moses and Aaron at the direct command of the LORD.
4.38: The Gershonite enrollment by clans and households —
4.39: every man between thirty and fifty fit for service —
4.40: came to two thousand six hundred and thirty.
4.41: These were the registered Gershonite servants — counted by Moses and Aaron as the LORD commanded.
4.42: The Merarite enrollment by clans and households —
4.43: every man between thirty and fifty fit for service —
4.44: came to three thousand two hundred.
4.45: These were the registered Merarite servants — counted by Moses and Aaron as the LORD commanded.
4.46: All the Levites enrolled by Moses, Aaron, and the tribal leaders — by clans and households —
4.47: every man between thirty and fifty capable of service and carrying in the tent of meeting —
4.48: came to eight thousand five hundred and eighty.
4.49: The LORD's command through Moses specified each man's duty and each man's load. Every assignment was made with divine precision, just as the LORD had instructed.

5.2: Give Israel this command: anyone with a skin disease, anyone with a bodily discharge, and anyone defiled by contact with the dead must be sent outside the camp.
5.3: Male and female alike — they must go outside. The camp is where I dwell; it must not be defiled.
5.4: Israel obeyed: the unclean were expelled from the camp, exactly as the LORD had said.
5.6: Tell the Israelites: when anyone — man or woman — commits a wrong against another person, they have also broken faith with the LORD. They bear that guilt.
5.7: Guilt requires a full public confession, then restoration: the full amount owed plus a twenty-percent premium, paid directly to the injured party.
5.8: If the wronged party is dead and has no surviving heir, the restitution goes to the LORD — paid through the priest — along with the atonement ram. The wrong must still be righted.
5.9: Every offering that Israelites bring to the priest — every holy gift — becomes the priest's.
5.10: What you designate as sacred is yours to give; once you give it to the priest, it is fully his.
5.12: Tell the Israelites: if a wife turns aside and acts faithlessly against her husband —
5.13: if she has been intimate with another man, undetected, with no witness and no evidence to bring the matter to light —
5.14: if a surge of jealous suspicion overtakes the husband — whether his wife has in fact been unfaithful or whether she is innocent —
5.15: then the husband must bring his wife to the priest, along with an offering: a tenth of an ephah of barley flour — no oil, no incense. It is the jealousy offering, a memorial offering that summons hidden sin into God's sight.
5.16: The priest brings the woman forward and places her before the LORD.
5.17: He takes sacred water in a clay jar, adds dust from the tabernacle floor — earth from God's holy dwelling mixed into the water of judgment.
5.18: The priest presents the woman before the LORD and loosens her hair — a sign of the accused standing exposed before divine judgment. Her hands hold the jealousy offering; the priest holds the bitter, curse-bearing water.
5.19: The priest then speaks the oath over her: 'If you are innocent — if no man has been with you, if you have not wandered into defilement — then this bitter water will not harm you. Be free.'
5.20: But if you have been unfaithful — if another man has been with you while you were bound to your husband —
5.21: then the priest pronounces the curse: 'May the LORD make you an example and a byword among your people — your body bearing the visible mark of the faithlessness you have hidden.'
5.22: May this water enter you and do what guilt demands.' The woman must respond: 'Amen, amen' — agreeing to stand under the verdict, whatever it may be.
5.23: The priest writes the curse on a scroll, then dissolves the ink into the bitter water — making the woman drink the written judgment.
5.24: She drinks the water. The curse-bearing judgment enters her.
5.25: The priest receives the jealousy offering from her hands, waves it before the LORD, and carries it to the altar.
5.26: A handful is burned on the altar as the token offering; then the woman drinks the water.
5.27: If she is guilty, the water works: bitter pain takes hold, her body manifests the judgment, and she becomes a public warning to her community. The hidden sin is brought into the open by God himself.
5.28: But if she is innocent, the water leaves her unharmed. She goes free — cleared by the same ordeal that would have condemned her, and given back the promise of children.
5.29: This is the ruling for cases of suspected marital unfaithfulness —
5.30: when jealous suspicion seizes a husband. He brings his wife before the LORD, the priest applies the full procedure, and the verdict belongs to God alone.
5.31: The husband is cleared by bringing the matter to God. The woman stands accountable for whatever she has done — or has not done, in which case she too goes free.

6.2: Tell the Israelites: when a man or woman takes a Nazirite vow — consecrating themselves in a special way to the LORD —
6.3: they must abstain completely from anything vine-related — no wine, no beer, no grape juice, no fresh grapes, no raisins. The vine is off-limits entirely.
6.4: For the full duration of the vow, nothing from the vine — not even the seeds or the outer skin.
6.5: No razor touches his head for the entire vow period. He is holy to the LORD; his uncut hair is the visible sign of that consecration, growing freely as his dedication to God.
6.6: During the Nazirite period, he must not approach any corpse — not even a family member's.
6.7: Even for a father, mother, brother, or sister who dies — he does not defile himself. The sacred vow-crown is on his head; that takes precedence over even the most binding family obligation.
6.8: For the whole duration of the vow, he is completely holy — set apart for the LORD.
6.9: But if someone suddenly dies beside him — defiling the Nazirite's consecrated head through no intention of his own — he must shave his head on the seventh day, the day of his purification.
6.10: On the eighth day he comes to the priest at the tent of meeting with two turtledoves or two young pigeons.
6.11: The priest offers one for sin and one as a burnt offering, making atonement for the accidental defilement. The Nazirite's head is re-consecrated the same day.
6.12: He begins the vow period again from scratch, bringing a guilt offering to cover the interruption. The days before the defilement are forfeited — the vow restarts.
6.13: When the Nazirite's full period of dedication is complete, this is the concluding ritual: he comes to the tent of meeting.
6.14: He brings the LORD a complete offering: a yearling lamb as a burnt offering, a yearling ewe as a sin offering, and a ram as a fellowship offering — all without defect.
6.15: A basket of unleavened bread — both the mixed loaves and the oil-brushed wafers — comes with the appropriate grain and drink offerings.
6.16: The priest presents everything before the LORD and offers the sin offering and the burnt offering.
6.17: Then the ram becomes the fellowship offering to the LORD — the communal meal sacrifice — accompanied by the unleavened bread, grain offering, and drink offering.
6.18: At the tent entrance, the Nazirite shaves off the hair that has been growing throughout his vow — the visible sign of his consecration — and places it on the fire of the fellowship sacrifice.
6.19: The priest takes the cooked shoulder of the ram plus one loaf and one wafer from the unleavened basket and places them in the Nazirite's own hands.
6.20: With the Nazirite's hands holding the food, the priest waves everything before the LORD — a joint presentation. These portions go to the priest; the vow is complete, and the Nazirite is free to drink wine again.
6.21: This is the governing rule for anyone who takes the Nazirite vow: the specified offerings are the minimum; he may bring more according to his means. He has vowed, and the vow must be fulfilled to the letter.
6.23: Say this to Aaron and his sons: This is the blessing you shall pronounce over Israel — these exact words:
6.24: May the LORD bless you and keep you safe —
6.25: may the LORD make his face shine toward you and show you his grace —
6.26: may the LORD lift his face toward you and give you wholeness and peace.
6.27: By this blessing they place my name upon my people — and I myself will bless them.

7.1: The day Moses completed the tabernacle's erection — anointing every piece, consecrating altar and furnishings alike —
7.2: the twelve tribal leaders came forward: one from each tribe, head of his ancestral house, the man responsible for his tribe's census registration.
7.3: Twelve oxen, six covered wagons: one ox per leader, one wagon per pair. They brought them all before the LORD at the tabernacle — a practical gift for holy work.
7.5: Accept these gifts for the tent of meeting's transport. Distribute them to the Levites, each group receiving what matches the weight of its assignment.
7.6: Moses accepted the wagons and oxen and apportioned them to the Levites.
7.7: The Gershonites — charged with carrying the tabernacle's woven curtains and coverings — received two wagons and four oxen.
7.8: The Merarites — carrying the tabernacle's heavier framework of boards, bars, and bases — received four wagons and eight oxen, supervised by Ithamar son of Aaron.
7.9: The Kohathites received no wagons. The holiest objects — ark, table, lampstand, altars — could not be carted; they were to be borne on human shoulders, carried close.
7.10: As the altar was anointed, all twelve leaders came forward with dedication offerings — each one presenting his tribute before the newly consecrated altar.
7.11: The LORD told Moses: give each leader his own day — twelve offerings over twelve days, one tribe at a time, the altar's dedication unhurried and complete.
7.12: Day one: Nahshon son of Amminadab, leader of Judah.
7.13: One silver dish of 130 shekels, one silver basin of 70 shekels — sanctuary weight, both filled with fine flour blended with oil as a grain offering;
7.14: one gold ladle of 10 shekels, filled with incense;
7.15: one bull, one ram, one yearling lamb — all for the burnt offering;
7.16: one male goat — the sin offering;
7.17: and for the fellowship offering: two bulls, five rams, five male goats, five yearling lambs. This was Nahshon son of Amminadab's complete offering.
7.18: Day two: Nethaneel son of Zuar, leader of Issachar.
7.19: One silver dish of 130 shekels, one silver basin of 70 shekels — sanctuary weight, both filled with fine flour blended with oil as a grain offering;
7.20: one gold ladle of 10 shekels, filled with incense;
7.21: one bull, one ram, one yearling lamb — all for the burnt offering;
7.22: one male goat — the sin offering;
7.23: and for the fellowship offering: two bulls, five rams, five male goats, five yearling lambs. This was Nethaneel son of Zuar's complete offering.
7.24: Day three: Eliab son of Helon, leader of Zebulun.
7.25: One silver dish of 130 shekels, one silver basin of 70 shekels — sanctuary weight, both filled with fine flour blended with oil as a grain offering;
7.26: one gold ladle of 10 shekels, filled with incense;
7.27: one bull, one ram, one yearling lamb — all for the burnt offering;
7.28: one male goat — the sin offering;
7.29: and for the fellowship offering: two bulls, five rams, five male goats, five yearling lambs. This was Eliab son of Helon's complete offering.
7.30: Day four: Elizur son of Shedeur, leader of Reuben.
7.31: One silver dish of 130 shekels, one silver basin of 70 shekels — sanctuary weight, both filled with fine flour blended with oil as a grain offering;
7.32: one gold ladle of 10 shekels, filled with incense;
7.33: one bull, one ram, one yearling lamb — all for the burnt offering;
7.34: one male goat — the sin offering;
7.35: and for the fellowship offering: two bulls, five rams, five male goats, five yearling lambs. This was Elizur son of Shedeur's complete offering.
7.36: Day five: Shelumiel son of Zurishaddai, leader of Simeon.
7.37: One silver dish of 130 shekels, one silver basin of 70 shekels — sanctuary weight, both filled with fine flour blended with oil as a grain offering;
7.38: one gold ladle of 10 shekels, filled with incense;
7.39: one bull, one ram, one yearling lamb — all for the burnt offering;
7.40: one male goat — the sin offering;
7.41: and for the fellowship offering: two bulls, five rams, five male goats, five yearling lambs. This was Shelumiel son of Zurishaddai's complete offering.
7.42: Day six: Eliasaph son of Deuel, leader of Gad.
7.43: One silver dish of 130 shekels, one silver basin of 70 shekels — sanctuary weight, both filled with fine flour blended with oil as a grain offering;
7.44: one gold ladle of 10 shekels, filled with incense;
7.45: one bull, one ram, one yearling lamb — all for the burnt offering;
7.46: one male goat — the sin offering;
7.47: and for the fellowship offering: two bulls, five rams, five male goats, five yearling lambs. This was Eliasaph son of Deuel's complete offering.
7.48: Day seven: Elishama son of Ammihud, leader of Ephraim.
7.49: One silver dish of 130 shekels, one silver basin of 70 shekels — sanctuary weight, both filled with fine flour blended with oil as a grain offering;
7.50: one gold ladle of 10 shekels, filled with incense;
7.51: one bull, one ram, one yearling lamb — all for the burnt offering;
7.52: one male goat — the sin offering;
7.53: and for the fellowship offering: two bulls, five rams, five male goats, five yearling lambs. This was Elishama son of Ammihud's complete offering.
7.54: Day eight: Gamaliel son of Pedahzur, leader of Manasseh.
7.55: One silver dish of 130 shekels, one silver basin of 70 shekels — sanctuary weight, both filled with fine flour blended with oil as a grain offering;
7.56: one gold ladle of 10 shekels, filled with incense;
7.57: one bull, one ram, one yearling lamb — all for the burnt offering;
7.58: one male goat — the sin offering;
7.59: and for the fellowship offering: two bulls, five rams, five male goats, five yearling lambs. This was Gamaliel son of Pedahzur's complete offering.
7.60: Day nine: Abidan son of Gideoni, leader of Benjamin.
7.61: One silver dish of 130 shekels, one silver basin of 70 shekels — sanctuary weight, both filled with fine flour blended with oil as a grain offering;
7.62: one gold ladle of 10 shekels, filled with incense;
7.63: one bull, one ram, one yearling lamb — all for the burnt offering;
7.64: one male goat — the sin offering;
7.65: and for the fellowship offering: two bulls, five rams, five male goats, five yearling lambs. This was Abidan son of Gideoni's complete offering.
7.66: Day ten: Ahiezer son of Ammishaddai, leader of Dan.
7.67: One silver dish of 130 shekels, one silver basin of 70 shekels — sanctuary weight, both filled with fine flour blended with oil as a grain offering;
7.68: one gold ladle of 10 shekels, filled with incense;
7.69: one bull, one ram, one yearling lamb — all for the burnt offering;
7.70: one male goat — the sin offering;
7.71: and for the fellowship offering: two bulls, five rams, five male goats, five yearling lambs. This was Ahiezer son of Ammishaddai's complete offering.
7.72: Day eleven: Pagiel son of Ocran, leader of Asher.
7.73: One silver dish of 130 shekels, one silver basin of 70 shekels — sanctuary weight, both filled with fine flour blended with oil as a grain offering;
7.74: one gold ladle of 10 shekels, filled with incense;
7.75: one bull, one ram, one yearling lamb — all for the burnt offering;
7.76: one male goat — the sin offering;
7.77: and for the fellowship offering: two bulls, five rams, five male goats, five yearling lambs. This was Pagiel son of Ocran's complete offering.
7.78: Day twelve: Ahira son of Enan, leader of Naphtali.
7.79: One silver dish of 130 shekels, one silver basin of 70 shekels — sanctuary weight, both filled with fine flour blended with oil as a grain offering;
7.80: one gold ladle of 10 shekels, filled with incense;
7.81: one bull, one ram, one yearling lamb — all for the burnt offering;
7.82: one male goat — the sin offering;
7.83: and for the fellowship offering: two bulls, five rams, five male goats, five yearling lambs. This was Ahira son of Enan's complete offering — the last of the twelve.
7.84: The twelve-day dedication of the altar yielded this: twelve silver dishes, twelve silver basins, twelve gold incense ladles — one set per tribe, identical in every case.
7.85: Each dish: 130 shekels; each basin: 70 shekels. Total silver: 2,400 sanctuary shekels — a precise accounting before the LORD.
7.86: Twelve gold ladles at 10 shekels each, all filled with incense: 120 shekels of gold total — the fragrance of twelve tribes rising before God.
7.87: The burnt offerings in total: twelve bulls, twelve rams, twelve yearling lambs with accompanying grain offerings. Sin offerings: twelve male goats — one tribe's guilt, one tribe's atonement, twelve times over.
7.88: The fellowship offerings in total: 24 bulls, 60 rams, 60 male goats, 60 yearling lambs. This was the complete dedication of the altar. Twelve days, twelve tribes, one consecrated altar — Israel united in worship.
7.89: When Moses entered the tent of meeting to speak with God, he heard the voice coming from above the mercy seat — from between the two cherubim over the ark of the covenant. The LORD spoke to him from that place, face to presence.

8.2: Tell Aaron: when he mounts the seven lamps, they are to cast their light forward — illuminating the space before the lampstand itself.
8.3: Aaron obeyed — he arranged the lamps to shine forward, exactly as the LORD had commanded Moses.
8.4: The lampstand was hammered gold throughout — shaft to blossoms, every part beaten from a single piece — exactly matching the design the LORD had shown Moses on the mountain.
8.6: Separate the Levites from the rest of Israel and purify them.
8.7: The purification rite: sprinkle cleansing water over them, have them shave their entire body, wash their garments — and they will be clean.
8.8: They are then to bring two young bulls: one as a burnt offering with a fine-flour-and-oil grain offering alongside it, and one as a sin offering.
8.9: Bring the Levites to the tent of meeting entrance and gather all Israel around them.
8.10: Present the Levites before the LORD. Then the whole Israelite assembly shall lay their hands on the Levites —
8.11: and Aaron shall present them to the LORD as a wave offering on Israel's behalf — a living gift dedicated to the LORD's service.
8.12: The Levites press their hands on the bulls' heads, transferring guilt. One bull becomes the sin offering, one the burnt offering — atonement for the Levites before they enter service.
8.13: Station the Levites before Aaron and his sons and lift them as a dedicated offering to the LORD.
8.14: This is how the Levites are formally distinguished from the rest of Israel — they belong to the LORD.
8.15: After the purification and the presentation, the Levites may take up their service at the tent of meeting.
8.16: The Levites belong entirely to me. I have taken them as a substitute for every firstborn son in Israel — every firstborn who opened a womb in the land.
8.17: Every Israelite firstborn — human and animal — belongs to me. The day I struck Egypt's firstborn, I consecrated Israel's firstborn as my own.
8.18: Now the Levites stand in the place of those firstborns — given to me instead.
8.19: I give the Levites to Aaron and his sons as a gift — assigned from Israel to serve Israel at the tent of meeting and to provide atonement, so that approaching the holy sanctuary does not cost Israel their lives.
8.20: Moses, Aaron, and the entire community of Israel carried out everything the LORD had commanded regarding the Levites — without exception.
8.21: The Levites underwent purification, washed their garments, and Aaron waved them before the LORD and made atonement for them. They were clean.
8.22: From that day on, the Levites took up their duties at the tent of meeting under Aaron and his sons — everything performed exactly as the LORD had commanded.
8.24: The Levites begin active service at age twenty-five — from that point they join the workforce of the tent of meeting.
8.25: At fifty they retire from the strenuous labor and serve no more in that capacity.
8.26: They may still assist their fellow Levites — helping, advising, keeping watch — but the heavy lifting is done. This is the Levites' life cycle of service.

9.1: In the first month of the second year out of Egypt — still in the Sinai wilderness — the LORD spoke to Moses:
9.2: The Israelites are to observe the Passover at its appointed time.
9.3: On the fourteenth day of this month, at twilight, observe it — every rite, every ordinance, exactly as established.
9.4: Moses relayed this to the Israelites, and they prepared to keep the Passover.
9.5: On the fourteenth day of the first month, at twilight, in the Sinai wilderness, the Israelites kept the Passover — every detail as the LORD had commanded.
9.6: Some men, however, were impure from contact with a human corpse and could not join the Passover. They came to Moses and Aaron that day —
9.7: and said: 'We are impure from handling the dead. Why should we be shut out from offering to the LORD at the time appointed for all Israel?'
9.8: Moses said: 'Wait. I will take your case to the LORD and hear what he says.'
9.10: Tell Israel: if anyone is impure from a corpse, or is on a long journey at Passover time, they are not simply excluded — they may still keep the Passover to the LORD.
9.11: They observe it one month later: the fourteenth of the second month, at twilight — with unleavened bread and bitter herbs, the full Passover meal.
9.12: No leftovers into morning, no broken bones — the make-up Passover follows every regulation of the original.
9.13: But a person who is clean and not traveling and simply neglects the Passover — that person is cut off from Israel. Skipping the appointed offering without cause is a rejection of the covenant; the guilt rests on him alone.
9.14: If a foreigner living among Israel wants to keep the Passover to the LORD, let him — but he must observe every regulation of it. One law, one table: resident alien and native-born stand equal before this feast.
9.15: The day the tabernacle was erected, the cloud settled over it — the tent housing the covenant. From evening onward it appeared as fire, glowing through the night until dawn.
9.16: This was constant: cloud by day, fire by night — the LORD's visible presence, never absent.
9.17: When the cloud lifted, Israel moved. When the cloud settled, Israel stopped and made camp. The cloud was their compass and their command.
9.18: March and rest — both were ordered by the LORD. As long as the cloud stayed, they stayed; when it moved, they moved.
9.19: When the cloud lingered for many days, Israel held its position — keeping watch, keeping faith, going nowhere until the LORD said otherwise.
9.20: When the cloud stayed only a few days, they stayed a few days — then moved at the LORD's signal. Short stop or long stop: always his call.
9.21: Even a cloud that settled at evening and lifted by morning was command enough — they broke camp and marched. Day departure or night departure: they followed.
9.22: Two days, a month, a year — length made no difference. Israel waited as long as the cloud stayed, then moved the moment it lifted. Their calendar was the presence of God.
9.23: Camp and march — always at the LORD's command, always through Moses. This was the discipline of a people learning to be led by God.

10.2: Make two trumpets of hammered silver — for calling the assembly to gather and for signaling the camps to march.
10.3: Both trumpets sounding together: the signal for the entire assembly to gather at the tent of meeting entrance.
10.4: One trumpet only: the leaders assemble, not the whole people. Different calls carry different summons.
10.5: A blast alarm: the eastern camps break camp and march.
10.6: A second alarm: the southern camps march. Each departure from camp is announced this way.
10.7: When gathering the assembly — not departing, just meeting — blow the trumpets without the alarm pattern.
10.8: The trumpets belong to Aaron's sons — only priests may sound them. This is a permanent ordinance through every generation.
10.9: When you go to war in your land and face an enemy pressing down on you — sound the alarm. The sound will rise before the LORD your God; he will remember you and you will be delivered.
10.10: Blow the trumpets also on joyful days — festivals, new moon celebrations — over the burnt offerings and fellowship sacrifices. The sound announces your worship before God and calls his attention to you. I am the LORD your God.
10.11: On the twentieth day of the second month of the second year, the cloud over the tabernacle rose. It was time to leave Sinai.
10.12: Israel marched out of the Sinai wilderness in ordered stages, the cloud moving ahead of them until it settled in the Paran wilderness.
10.13: This was Israel's first march since the tabernacle's completion — ordered by the LORD through Moses.
10.14: Judah's division led the march, regiment by regiment. Nahshon son of Amminadab at the head.
10.15: Issachar's regiment followed, under Nethaneel son of Zuar.
10.16: Then Zebulun, under Eliab son of Helon.
10.17: Behind Judah came the Gershonites and Merarites, bearing the dismantled tabernacle — the structure moving with the people.
10.18: Next: Reuben's division, Elizur son of Shedeur commanding.
10.19: Simeon followed, under Shelumiel son of Zurishaddai.
10.20: Then Gad, under Eliasaph son of Deuel.
10.21: The Kohathites marched in the middle of the column carrying the sanctuary's sacred objects — ark, altars, lampstand. The Gershonites and Merarites ahead of them would have the tabernacle structure erected before the Kohathites arrived with the furnishings.
10.22: Ephraim's division came next — Elishama son of Ammihud leading.
10.23: Manasseh followed, under Gamaliel son of Pedahzur.
10.24: Benjamin followed, under Abidan son of Gideoni.
10.25: Dan's division brought up the rear — the rear guard of the entire column — Ahiezer son of Ammishaddai commanding.
10.26: Asher followed, under Pagiel son of Ocran.
10.27: Naphtali last, under Ahira son of Enan — and so the column closed.
10.28: This was Israel's order of march — twelve tribes in sequence, a people on the move with their God.
10.29: Moses said to Hobab — son of Raguel, his kinsman by marriage — 'We are moving toward the land the LORD promised to give us. Come with us. We will treat you well, for the LORD has spoken good things over Israel.'
10.30: Hobab said: 'I will not come. I am going back to my own land, my own people.'
10.31: Moses pressed him: 'Do not leave us, please. You know this wilderness — where to camp, where water is, what dangers lie ahead. You would be our eyes.'
10.32: Come with us. Whatever blessing the LORD brings upon us, you will share in it.
10.33: They departed from Sinai — the mountain of the LORD — and traveled three days. The ark of the covenant went ahead of them those three days, scouting their resting place.
10.34: By day, the LORD's cloud spread over them as they marched — shelter and presence both.
10.35: Each time the ark lifted and Israel began to march, Moses would call out: 'Rise, LORD! Let your enemies scatter! Let those who hate you turn and run before you!'
10.36: And when the ark settled and the camp was made, Moses said: 'Return, LORD — to the ten thousand thousands of Israel.' The prayers framed every march.

11.1: The people began to complain — and the LORD heard it and his anger flared. Fire broke out from the LORD and burned along the edges of the camp, consuming those who had voiced the complaint.
11.2: The people cried to Moses. Moses prayed to the LORD, and the fire died.
11.3: The place was named Taberah — Burning — because the LORD's fire had burned there.
11.4: The mixed crowd among them gave way to craving — and the craving spread. The Israelites wept and demanded: 'Who will give us meat?'
11.5: They remembered: 'In Egypt we had fish — free fish. Cucumbers, melons, leeks, onions, garlic —'
11.6: — 'and now our appetite has shriveled. There is nothing. Nothing except this manna, every day, nothing but manna.'
11.7: The manna was white as bdellium resin, small and round as coriander seed.
11.8: People gathered it each morning, ground it in mills or crushed it in mortars, cooked it in pots or baked it into flat cakes. It tasted of rich olive oil. It was bread from heaven — and they were tired of it.
11.9: It appeared with the dew every night, covering the camp by morning.
11.10: Moses heard the sound of weeping rising from every family, from the entrance of every tent — a camp of grief and complaint. The LORD's anger blazed, and Moses himself was stricken.
11.11: Moses cried out to the LORD: 'Why have you done this to me — your servant? What have I done to lose your favor? Why have you put the weight of all this people on my shoulders?'
11.12: 'Did I conceive this people? Did I give birth to them — that you tell me to carry them in my arms like a nursing mother carries an infant, all the way to the land you promised their fathers?'
11.13: 'Where am I supposed to get meat for six hundred thousand people who are weeping in my face demanding to be fed?'
11.14: 'I cannot carry this people alone. The weight is crushing me.'
11.15: 'If this is what you intend for me — if I truly have found favor with you — then kill me now. Do not let me watch myself fail. Death is better than this.'
11.16: The LORD responded to Moses — not with rebuke but with a solution: 'Gather seventy men, known elders and officers of Israel, and bring them to the tent of meeting. They will stand with you.'
11.17: 'I will come down and speak with you there. I will take of the Spirit that rests on you and distribute it among them — and they will share the burden of leading this people, so you are not carrying it alone.'
11.18: 'And tell the people: prepare yourselves for tomorrow — you will eat meat. You have wept in the LORD's ears, insisting Egypt was better. Fine. The LORD will give you what you demand.'
11.19: 'Not one day, not two, not five, not ten, not twenty —'
11.20: '— but a full month, until meat is coming out of your nostrils and you are sick of it. Because you have despised the LORD who is living among you and wept as though Egypt was life and Sinai was death.'
11.21: Moses pressed back: 'Six hundred thousand fighting men — not counting women and children. You are saying you will give all of them meat for a solid month?'
11.22: 'Slaughter every flock and herd — would that be enough? Drain the sea of its fish — would that be enough?'
11.23: The LORD answered Moses: 'Is my arm too short? You will see today whether what I say happens or not.'
11.24: Moses went out, relayed the LORD's words to the people, and then gathered the seventy elders and positioned them in a circle around the tent of meeting.
11.25: The LORD came down in the cloud and spoke with Moses. He took of the Spirit that rested on Moses and distributed it among the seventy elders. When the Spirit came to rest on each of them, they prophesied — a single outpouring, a sign that the Spirit had come.
11.26: Two of the seventy — Eldad and Medad — had not come out to the tent. They were on the list; the Spirit came to them where they were. They prophesied right there in the camp.
11.27: A young man ran to Moses: 'Eldad and Medad — they're prophesying in the camp!'
11.28: Joshua son of Nun — Moses' aide from his youth — spoke up: 'My lord Moses, put a stop to this!'
11.29: Moses said: 'Are you protecting my reputation? Don't. I wish all the LORD's people were prophets — that the LORD would pour out his Spirit on every one of them!'
11.30: Moses and the elders returned to the camp.
11.31: A great wind from the LORD swept quails in from the sea and dumped them around the camp — a day's journey out in every direction, lying thick on the ground to a depth of about three feet.
11.32: All day, all night, all the next day — the people gathered quails in a frenzy. Even the smallest haul was ten homers. They spread them out around the camp to dry in the sun.
11.33: The meat was still in their mouths — not yet swallowed — when the LORD's anger broke over the camp. He struck the people with a devastating plague.
11.34: The place was named Kibroth-hattaavah — Graves of Craving — because the people who craved were buried there.
11.35: From Graves of Craving, Israel moved on to Hazeroth, where they stopped and made camp.

12.1: Miriam and Aaron turned on Moses over his Cushite wife — he had married a Cushite woman, and they made it the occasion for a broader challenge to his authority.
12.2: 'Has the LORD spoken only through Moses? Has he not spoken through us as well?' The LORD heard every word.
12.3: Moses himself said nothing. The text pauses to note: Moses was the most humble man on earth — and his silence here is that humility on display.
12.4: Suddenly the LORD spoke — to all three at once: 'The three of you — come to the tent of meeting. Now.' All three went.
12.5: The LORD descended in a pillar of cloud and took his place at the tent entrance. He called Aaron and Miriam forward — and they came.
12.6: 'Listen to me. When I have a prophet among my people, I make myself known in visions, I speak in dreams.'
12.7: 'Not so with my servant Moses. He is faithful in my entire household — trusted with everything.'
12.8: 'With Moses I speak directly — mouth to mouth, face to presence, plainly and not in riddles. He has seen the very form of the LORD. How then did you dare speak against him?'
12.9: The LORD's anger burned against them — and he left.
12.10: As the cloud lifted from the tent, Miriam was struck — a severe skin disease, white as snow. Aaron turned and saw her.
12.11: Aaron turned to Moses: 'My lord — please — do not hold this sin against us. We acted like fools; we have sinned.'
12.12: 'Do not let her be like a stillborn child — flesh already half consumed at the moment of birth.'
12.13: Moses cried out to the LORD: 'God — please! Heal her!'
12.14: The LORD said to Moses: 'If her father had only spat in her face, she would bear the shame seven days. She is to be shut outside the camp seven days. After that, she is received back in.'
12.15: Miriam was shut outside the camp seven days. Israel did not march. The entire people waited — seven days — until Miriam was restored.
12.16: Then Israel moved on from Hazeroth and made camp in the Paran wilderness.

31.1: Then the LORD said to Moses,
31.2: "Exact Israel's vengeance on the Midianites — this is the last task before you are taken home to your ancestors."
31.3: Moses addressed the people: "Equip a fighting force from your number. They will march against Midian and execute the LORD's own vengeance."
31.4: Draw a thousand men from each tribe — every tribe equal, every tribe accountable — to make up the force.
31.5: Twelve thousand warriors, a thousand from each tribe, were mustered and sent into battle.
31.6: Moses sent the force — twelve thousand strong — with Phinehas son of Eleazar serving as the priestly representative, bearing the sacred implements and the alarm trumpets.
31.7: They struck Midian exactly as the LORD had ordered — every man was put to the sword.
31.8: Among the fallen were Midian's five kings: Evi, Rekem, Zur, Hur, and Reba. Balaam son of Beor — the seer who set the Peor trap — was also cut down.
31.9: Israel's army took the women and children prisoner, driving off every herd, every flock, every possession as spoil.
31.10: Every city, every encampment Midian had occupied was put to the torch.
31.11: Everything was taken: every prisoner, every animal, every article of value.
31.12: The army returned to camp on the Moabite plains by the Jordan at Jericho, bringing before Moses, Eleazar, and the whole assembly every prisoner, every animal, and every piece of plunder.
31.13: Moses, Eleazar, and all the tribal leaders marched out beyond the camp to receive the returning army.
31.14: Moses met them with fury. He turned on the officers — every commander of a thousand, every commander of a hundred who had come back from the field.
31.15: "You kept the women alive?" Moses demanded.
31.16: "These are the very women who, at Balaam's instruction, lured Israel into the Baal-worship at Peor. That faithlessness triggered the plague that struck down thousands in the LORD's own congregation."
31.17: "Execute every male child. Execute every woman who has been with a man."
31.18: "Only the girls who have never been with a man may be kept alive."
31.19: "All who have killed or touched the dead are now ritually impure. Remain outside the camp for seven days, purifying yourselves and your captives on the third day and the seventh."
31.20: "Everything you brought back from battle — clothing, leather goods, woven fabrics, wooden vessels — must be purified."
31.21: Eleazar spoke to the veterans as they waited outside the camp: "Here is what the LORD's law requires regarding what you have brought back:"
31.23: "— whatever survives fire must be passed through fire, then rinsed with the purification water. Whatever fire would destroy must be washed in water instead."
31.24: "On the seventh day wash all your clothing. Once you are clean, you may return to the community."
31.26: "Inventory everything captured — every person, every animal — you and Eleazar and the tribal heads together."
31.27: "Split the spoil in half: one portion to the fighting men, the other to the whole community."
31.28: "From the soldiers' share, one in five hundred of every category — people, cattle, donkeys, sheep — belongs to the LORD as tribute."
31.29: "Draw that amount from their share and present it to Eleazar as an offering lifted to the LORD."
31.30: "From the community's share, one in fifty of everything — persons and animals alike — goes to the Levites who serve at the LORD's tabernacle."
31.31: Moses and Eleazar carried out everything the LORD had ordered.
31.32: The total inventory of what the army had captured: six hundred seventy-five thousand sheep;
31.35: thirty-two thousand young women who had not known a man.
31.36: The soldiers' share: three hundred thirty-seven thousand five hundred sheep;
31.37: the LORD's levy from the sheep: six hundred seventy-five;
31.38: thirty-six thousand cattle, the LORD's portion being seventy-two;
31.39: thirty thousand five hundred donkeys, sixty-one for the LORD;
31.40: sixteen thousand persons, thirty-two of whom were designated the LORD's tribute.
31.41: Moses handed over the LORD's entire allotted portion to Eleazar, exactly as he had been instructed.
31.42: From the half allocated to the community at large,
31.43: the community received: three hundred thirty-seven thousand five hundred sheep,
31.47: From Israel's portion, Moses took one in fifty of persons and animals and gave them to the Levites who serve the tabernacle — following the LORD's explicit command to the letter.
31.48: Then the entire officer corps — every commander of a thousand, every commander of a hundred — came forward to Moses.
31.49: They said: "We have mustered every man who served under us. Not one is missing."
31.50: "So we have brought to the LORD a voluntary offering: the gold jewelry each of us took as personal spoil — armlets, bracelets, signet rings, earrings, pendants — to atone for our own souls before the LORD. No command required. We offer it because every man came home."
31.51: Moses and Eleazar accepted it all — every piece of crafted gold.
31.52: The gold those officers brought before the LORD totaled sixteen thousand seven hundred fifty shekels — a massive spontaneous offering.
31.53: The common soldiers, unlike their officers, had kept their personal plunder without offering it — which was within their rights.
31.54: Moses and Eleazar carried every gram of that donated gold into the tent of meeting — a memorial laid before the LORD on behalf of all Israel, testifying that the LORD had brought every man home.

32.1: The tribes of Reuben and Gad owned enormous herds. When they surveyed Jazer and Gilead and saw prime grazing country,
32.2: they brought their case to Moses, Eleazar, and the tribal leadership.
32.3: "The country of Ataroth, Dibon, Jazer, Nimrah, Heshbon, Elealeh, Sebam, Nebo, and Beon —"
32.4: "— the very territory the LORD opened before Israel — it is rich grazing land, and we have herds to fill it."
32.5: "If we have found any favor with you, grant us this land as our holding. We ask not to be taken across the Jordan."
32.6: Moses's response was sharp: "Your brothers are going to war, and you want to stay behind?"
32.7: "Why would you drain the courage from all Israel — the resolve it takes to enter the land the LORD has promised?"
32.8: "Your ancestors pulled this same move when I sent them from Kadesh-barnea to reconnoiter the land."
32.9: "They reached the Eshcol valley, took one look, and came back to drain all hope from the congregation — turned the people's heart away from the land God was giving them."
32.10: "The LORD's anger ignited. He swore an oath:"
32.11: "'Not one man who left Egypt — no one twenty or older — will lay eyes on the land I promised Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. They refused to follow me with an undivided heart.'"
32.12: "'The only exceptions: Caleb son of Jephunneh and Joshua son of Nun. They gave the LORD their undivided loyalty.'"
32.13: "The LORD held Israel to that oath — forty years in the wilderness, until the entire faithless generation was spent and gone."
32.14: "And now here you stand: sons of the very men who failed. A second crop of faithlessness, poised to add more fuel to the LORD's righteous anger."
32.15: "If you defect — if you hold back — he will abandon Israel in the wilderness again, and every last life lost will be on your hands."
32.16: The two tribes stepped up with a counter-offer: "Let us build pens for our herds and walled towns for our families here —"
32.17: "— and we will arm ourselves and march at the front of Israel's column until every tribe is settled in its land. Our families will be secure in walled towns while we are fighting. We ask nothing for ourselves until Israel's task is complete."
32.18: "Not a single man of us comes home until every Israelite is standing on his own land."
32.19: "Our portion is here, east of the Jordan. We claim nothing west of the river — that belongs to the rest of Israel. We take only what is already ours."
32.20: Moses accepted the terms: "If you will do this — if you march armed before the LORD into the fight,"
32.21: "if every fighting man of yours crosses the Jordan before the LORD, pressing forward until he drives his enemies from the land —"
32.22: "— and when the land lies at rest before the LORD, then come back. You will have discharged every obligation, and this territory will be permanently yours under the LORD's authority."
32.23: "But if you fail — if you go back on your word — you will have sinned against the LORD. And sins of this kind do not stay hidden: they hunt you down."
32.24: "Go build your towns and your sheep pens. But hold to every word you have spoken."
32.25: The Gadites and Reubenites answered: "We will do exactly what you have commanded."
32.26: "Our families — children, wives, herds, and flocks — will stay in the Gilead towns."
32.27: "Every fighting man among us will cross over, fully armed, marching before the LORD — exactly as you have said."
32.28: Moses formalized the agreement, laying out its terms before Eleazar, Joshua, and the tribal chieftains of Israel.
32.29: "Should the Gadites and Reubenites cross fully armed and fight alongside you until the land is subdued — award them Gilead as their permanent holding."
32.30: "But if they renege — if they will not cross armed — they forfeit their separate claim and receive their portion in Canaan alongside the rest of Israel."
32.31: Gad and Reuben replied without hesitation: "Whatever the LORD has spoken — we will carry it out."
32.32: "We will march armed before the LORD into Canaan. Our inheritance, however, stays here east of the river — that is settled."
32.33: Moses awarded Gad, Reuben, and the half-tribe of Manasseh all the territory once held by Sihon king of the Amorites and Og king of Bashan — every city and its surrounding countryside.
32.34: The tribe of Gad set to work: they rebuilt Dibon, Ataroth, Aroer,
32.36: Beth-nimrah and Beth-haran — all walled and fortified, with pens for their herds.
32.38: Nebo, Baal-meon — renamed — and Sibmah; the rebuilt cities received new identities.
32.39: The clan of Machir son of Manasseh struck out toward Gilead, seized it, and expelled the Amorites who had held it.
32.40: Moses formalized the grant: Gilead to Machir — and Machir made it home.
32.41: Jair son of Manasseh swept through the surrounding settlements, took them all, and renamed the whole district Havvoth-jair — the Villages of Jair.
32.42: Nobah seized Kenath and its satellite towns, stamping his own name on the captured city.

33.1: Here is the full record of Israel's journey out of Egypt — every camp, every march, every station the LORD's people passed through under Moses and Aaron.
33.2: At the LORD's direction Moses wrote it all down — a record of every departure, every leg of the journey, preserved in order.
33.3: They marched out of Rameses on the fifteenth of the first month — the morning after the Passover — in full view of Egypt, openly, under no concealment.
33.4: Behind them, Egypt was burying its dead — every firstborn the LORD had struck. Not a single Egyptian god had been able to protect its people.
33.6: Succoth to Etham, where the desert begins.
33.7: Etham to Pi-hahiroth — a seeming retreat, camping in front of Migdol with the sea before them.
33.8: From Hahiroth they walked into the sea and out the other side — then three days into the Etham wilderness to Marah, where the water was bitter.
33.9: Marah to Elim — twelve springs and seventy palms, a welcome rest after the bitter water.
33.10: Elim to the shore of the Red Sea.
33.11: The Red Sea to the wilderness of Sin.
33.14: Alush to Rephidim, where there was no water — where Moses struck the rock and water came.
33.15: Rephidim to Sinai — the mountain where the covenant was sealed.
33.16: Sinai to Kibroth-hattaavah — the Graves of Craving, named for those who died demanding meat.
33.17: Kibroth-hattaavah to Hazeroth — where Miriam's skin turned white as snow.
33.36: Ezion-geber to the wilderness of Zin — Kadesh, where the scouts' verdict condemned a generation.
33.37: Kadesh to Mount Hor, at Edom's boundary — where the high priesthood changed hands.
33.38: At the LORD's direct command, in the fortieth year — the first of the fifth month — Aaron the priest climbed Mount Hor and did not come down.
33.39: Aaron was a hundred and twenty-three years old. He died on the mountain.
33.40: Word reached the Canaanite king of Arad, ruling the Negeb, that Israel was on the move.
33.44: Oboth to Iye-abarim — the edge of Moab.
33.47: Almon-diblathaim to the Abarim heights — Nebo in view, the mountain Moses would climb at the end.
33.48: The Abarim mountains to the plains of Moab by the Jordan — and there the journey ended.
33.49: The camp spread from Beth-jeshimoth to Abel-shittim — the final staging ground, Canaan visible across the river.
33.50: There on the Moabite plains, with Canaan across the river, the LORD addressed Moses:
33.51: "Tell the people: The moment you cross the Jordan into Canaan,"
33.52: "drive out every inhabitant. Shatter every carved idol, melt every cast image, level every high place. Leave nothing standing that could become a snare."
33.53: "Occupy the land and make it your home — it is a gift from me, formally given, and it is yours."
33.54: "The land is apportioned by lot according to tribal size — proportionate, fair, decided by the LORD's own hand in the casting. Each tribe holds what the lot assigns. No tribe is favored; the LORD oversees the distribution."
33.55: "But leave the population intact, and they will be gravel in your eyes and splinters in your flesh — gnawing at you generation after generation in the very land I am giving you."
33.56: "Fail at this, and the judgment I reserved for them I will turn on you instead."

34.2: "Tell Israel: When you enter Canaan, the land I am assigning you as your permanent inheritance has these boundaries:"
34.3: "The southern boundary: from the wilderness of Zin, hugging Edom, east to the tip of the Salt Sea."
34.4: "Trace it: up the Scorpion Pass, across to Zin, south of Kadesh-barnea, on to Hazar-addar, then Azmon."
34.5: "From Azmon west to the Wadi of Egypt — and there the southern border meets the sea."
34.6: "The western boundary is the Great Sea. No drawn line needed — just coastline."
34.7: "Northern boundary: from the Great Sea to Mount Hor;"
34.8: "Mount Hor to the pass of Hamath, ending at Zedad;"
34.9: "on to Ziphron, then Hazar-enan — that is where the north ends."
34.11: "down from Shepham to Riblah on the east side of Ain, then south to the eastern shore of the Sea of Kinnereth;"
34.12: "then south along the Jordan to the Dead Sea. Within these lines — sea to salt sea, mountain to river — lies the land that is yours."
34.13: Moses addressed the assembly: "The entire territory just described is what the LORD has ordered apportioned among nine and a half tribes by lot."
34.14: "Reuben, Gad, and half of Manasseh are already settled east of the Jordan — they receive nothing west of the river."
34.15: "Those two and a half tribes are planted east of the river, the Jordan at their backs, facing the rising sun."
34.17: "Eleazar the priest and Joshua son of Nun will oversee the allotment."
34.18: "Each tribe will also designate one leader to serve on the allocation commission."
34.19: "The commissioners: Judah sends Caleb son of Jephunneh;"
34.28: "and Naphtali sends Pedahel son of Ammihud."
34.29: These twelve, under Eleazar and Joshua, are the LORD's appointed commissioners for dividing the promised land among his people.

35.1: The LORD spoke to Moses on the Moabite plains by the Jordan:
35.2: "When Israel takes possession of the land, each tribe must carve out towns from its inheritance and give them to the Levites to live in — with open pastureland surrounding each town."
35.3: "The towns are for the Levites' households; the pasturelands are for their herds. They receive no farmland, but they must have somewhere to live."
35.4: "Each town's pasture extends a thousand cubits out from the wall — a protective ring of open ground on all sides."
35.5: "Two thousand cubits on each of the four sides, the town at the center of that square — that is the pasture boundary."
35.6: "Six of the Levitical towns must be designated as cities of refuge — a protected destination for anyone who kills unintentionally. The remaining forty-two towns complete the Levites' allotment."
35.7: "Forty-eight towns in all, each with its pasture, drawn proportionally from every tribe — that is the Levites' civic inheritance in a land they will never personally farm."
35.8: "Proportion matters: the tribe that inherits the most gives the most towns; the smallest tribe gives fewer. The Levites draw from every tribe without overburdening any one of them."
35.10: "Tell Israel: Once you are across the Jordan,"
35.11: "establish cities of refuge — places a person can run to after killing someone without malice or premeditation."
35.12: "They exist to protect an accidental killer from the blood-avenger — to guarantee that no one is executed before the community has heard the full case."
35.13: "Six cities — that is the refuge network Israel is to maintain."
35.14: "Three east of the Jordan, three west — covering the whole extent of the land."
35.15: "The refuge is not for Israelites alone. The alien living in your midst, the passing stranger — anyone who kills without intent has the same right to flee, the same claim on the city's protection."
35.16: "Iron weapon, death — murder. There is no ambiguity, no refuge available. The killer must die."
35.17: "Stone large enough to kill, and the person dies — murder. The murderer must be executed."
35.18: "Wooden weapon, death — same verdict. The weapon's material is irrelevant; what matters is capacity to kill and the act of using it."
35.19: "The right to execute belongs to the blood-avenger — the nearest male kin of the victim. When he finds the killer outside any city of refuge, he may act."
35.20: "If the killing was driven by hatred — a shove from behind, a thrown weapon from hiding —"
35.21: "or struck down with bare hands in an act of hatred — that is murder. No refuge. The blood-avenger has the right to act on sight."
35.22: "Suppose the push was sudden, no hatred involved — a collision, an accident — or something thrown without any intent to harm:"
35.23: "suppose a heavy stone falls on someone with no premeditation, no prior enmity:"
35.24: "the community serves as court. The congregation hears both sides and applies these laws."
35.25: "If the community rules accidental killing, they protect the manslayer — escorting him back to his city of refuge. He stays there until the high priest dies. The high priest's death is his release."
35.26: "If the manslayer steps beyond the boundary of his assigned city —"
35.27: "— and the blood-avenger finds him there and kills him, the avenger has committed no crime. The manslayer's protection ended the moment he crossed the city line."
35.28: "His obligation is clear: stay within the city until the high priest dies. When that day comes, he walks free — back to his own land, untouchable by the avenger."
35.29: "This is settled law — permanent, applicable wherever Israel lives, binding on every generation."
35.30: "Execution requires witnesses — plural. One person's word alone cannot send someone to their death. This is Israel's protection against vengeance masquerading as justice."
35.31: "Blood money cannot buy a murderer's life. No ransom. The death penalty for deliberate homicide is not negotiable and not commutable by any payment."
35.32: "The manslayer cannot buy early release from his city. No payment, no exception, no early parole before the high priest's natural death. Both rules — no ransom for the murderer, no early exit for the manslayer — guard the same conviction: life is not a commodity."
35.33: "Murder defiles the land itself — not metaphorically but actually. The only purification for shed blood is the blood of the one who shed it. There is no ritual substitute, no offering that cleans what a murderer has stained."
35.34: "Here is the deepest reason: I live here. The LORD dwells among Israel. A defiled land is an affront to the holy God whose home it is. Keep it clean — not for your own sake alone, but because the one who inhabits it is holy."

36.1: The clan-heads of the Gilead line — Machir's family within Manasseh, Joseph's descendants — brought a case before Moses and the tribal leadership of Israel.
36.2: "The LORD ordered the land distributed by lot," they said. "And we accept that the LORD ordered Zelophehad's inheritance to pass to his daughters."
36.3: "But consider: if they marry into another tribe, their inherited land goes with them. Our tribal holding shrinks. The land the LORD gave us walks away through marriage."
36.4: "Not even the Jubilee restores it. The Jubilee returns land to its original family line — but if those daughters are now in another tribe, that is the family line the land returns to. Once lost through marriage, it is lost for good."
36.5: Moses received the LORD's ruling and announced it to the assembly: "The Josephite clans are right."
36.6: "Zelophehad's daughters may marry any man they choose — they are free. But they must choose from within their own tribe. That is the LORD's ruling."
36.7: "The principle: tribal inheritances do not cross tribal lines. Each tribe holds what it was given. This is what it means to be a people with a God-apportioned land."
36.8: "The same principle applies to every daughter who inherits in any tribe: marry within your father's clan. The land stays where it was planted."
36.9: "Israel's map must not be redrawn by marriage. Every tribe keeps its boundaries. The land the LORD assigned remains where he assigned it."
36.10: The daughters of Zelophehad complied without hesitation, doing precisely what the LORD had directed.
36.11: Mahlah, Tirzah, Hoglah, Milcah, Noah — all five daughters married within the Manasseh clan, choosing their cousins.
36.12: Their inheritance stayed in Manasseh — right where it began. The land did not move.
36.13: These were the final legal rulings the LORD delivered through Moses to Israel — spoken on the plains of Moab, within sight of the Jordan, on the threshold of the promised land. Here Numbers ends.

19.1: The LORD gave this charge to Moses and Aaron:
19.2: Here is the law's requirement that the LORD has commanded: Tell the Israelites to bring you a red heifer — unblemished, spotless, never broken to a yoke.
19.3: Give her to Eleazar the priest. She is to be led outside the camp and slaughtered there in his presence.
19.4: Eleazar takes some of her blood on his finger and sprinkles it seven times toward the tent of meeting.
19.5: The whole animal is burned before him — hide, flesh, blood, and all.
19.6: The priest throws cedar wood, hyssop, and scarlet thread into the blaze — the same materials used in other cleansing rites.
19.7: The officiating priest must wash his clothes and bathe — only after that may he re-enter the camp, though he remains ritually unclean until sunset. He who purifies others is rendered impure.
19.8: The man who burned her likewise washes his clothes and bathes; he too is unclean until sunset.
19.9: A ritually clean man gathers the ashes and deposits them outside the camp in a clean place. They are held in reserve — a standing supply of purification water for the whole congregation. This is a purification rite, not an atonement for moral sin but a remedy for the defilement of death.
19.10: Even the man who gathers the ashes must wash his clothes and remains unclean until sunset. This ordinance stands forever — it applies equally to native Israelites and to resident aliens among them.
19.11: Touch a dead person's body — anyone's — and you are unclean for seven full days.
19.12: He must undergo purification on the third day and again on the seventh — both applications are required. Skip the third-day rite and the seventh day brings no cleansing.
19.13: Touch a corpse and refuse purification, and you contaminate the LORD's own dwelling. The penalty is expulsion from Israel. The purification water was never applied; the defilement stands. He carries it into every sacred space he enters.
19.14: Here is the ruling for a death inside a dwelling: everyone who enters that tent, and everything inside it, is unclean for seven days.
19.15: Any container left uncovered — no lid tied down — is contaminated.
19.16: Out in the open — touch someone killed by the sword, any human corpse, a human bone, even a grave — and you incur seven days of uncleanness.
19.17: For the unclean person, take some of the heifer's ashes and add fresh spring water to them in a vessel.
19.18: A ritually clean person takes a bunch of hyssop, dips it in this water, and sprinkles everything contaminated — the tent itself, all the furnishings, every person who was there, and anyone who touched bone, slain man, corpse, or grave.
19.19: The sprinkling happens twice: third day and seventh day. On the seventh the person undergoing purification washes his clothes, bathes, and is fully clean by sunset.
19.20: The unclean person who refuses purification is cut off from the assembly. He has defiled the LORD's sanctuary. The cleansing rite was never performed; his defilement remains upon him.
19.21: This statute stands forever: the one who performs the sprinkling must wash his clothes afterward. Even contact with the purification water itself renders a person unclean until sunset — the paradox of a holy remedy that defiles its handler.
19.22: Uncleanness spreads by contact: whatever an unclean person handles becomes unclean; whoever then touches that object is unclean until sunset.

20.1: In the first month the whole community reached the Desert of Zin and camped at Kadesh. There Miriam died. There she was buried.
20.2: There was no water. The community turned on Moses and Aaron.
20.3: 'Better to have died alongside our kinsmen!' they railed at Moses. 'We would rather have fallen before the LORD with them!'
20.4: 'Why did you drag us — and our animals — into this wasteland to die?'
20.5: 'This is a place of nothing — no crops, no fruit, not even water. Why did you bring us out of Egypt for this?'
20.6: Moses and Aaron withdrew from the crowd to the entrance of the tent of meeting and fell prostrate. There the glory of the LORD appeared to them.
20.8: 'Take your staff. Gather everyone — you and Aaron together. Speak to the rock while they all watch. The rock will give its water, and you will provide drink for the congregation and their animals.'
20.9: Moses took the staff from before the LORD, as he was told.
20.10: 'Listen, you rebels!' Moses cried when the assembly had gathered at the rock. 'Are we to pull water out of this rock for you?' — claiming the act as his own, not God's.
20.11: Moses raised his hand and struck the rock — not once but twice. Water gushed out in abundance. The people drank, and so did their animals. But Moses had spoken when he should have spoken, and struck when he should have spoken.
20.12: 'You did not trust me,' the LORD told Moses and Aaron. 'You did not honor my holiness before Israel's eyes. For that, neither of you will lead this people into the land I am giving them.' The sentence was final.
20.13: These became the waters of Meribah — 'Strife.' Israel quarreled with the LORD here. And here, despite human failure, the LORD vindicated his own holiness.
20.14: From Kadesh, Moses sent word to Edom's king: 'Your brother Israel has a message for you. You know the suffering we have endured —'
20.15: 'our ancestors went down to Egypt; we lived there for generations. The Egyptians dealt harshly with us and our forebears,'
