20.16: 'We cried to the LORD, and he heard us. He sent his messenger and brought us out of Egypt. We stand now at Kadesh — right on your border.'
20.17: 'Give us passage through your land. We will not enter your fields or vineyards; we will not draw from your wells. We ask only for the main road — straight through, without straying, until we have cleared your borders.'
20.18: Edom's answer was blunt: 'You shall not pass. Try it, and we meet you with drawn swords.'
20.19: Israel pressed back: 'We will stay on the road. If any of us — person or animal — drinks from your water, we will pay the price. We are asking nothing more than passage.'
20.20: Edom remained unmoved: 'You shall not pass.' And Edom marched out with a great force to enforce that refusal.
20.21: Edom shut the door. Israel had to find another way.
20.22: The entire community broke camp from Kadesh and traveled to Mount Hor.
20.23: At Mount Hor — right on Edom's boundary — the LORD addressed Moses and Aaron:
20.24: 'Aaron's time has come. He will be gathered to his ancestors and will never set foot in the land I am giving Israel. At Meribah you both defied my word.'
20.25: 'Bring Aaron and his son Eleazar up the mountain.'
20.26: 'Take Aaron's vestments from him and place them on Eleazar. Aaron will die on that mountain and be gathered to his ancestors.'
20.27: Moses obeyed. They ascended Mount Hor in full view of the entire community.
20.28: There at the summit Moses removed Aaron's priestly robes and placed them on Eleazar. Aaron died at the top of the mountain. Moses and Eleazar came down alone.
20.29: When the people saw that Aaron was gone, all Israel mourned him for thirty days.

21.1: The Canaanite king of Arad ruled the Negev. When he heard Israel was approaching along the Atharim route, he struck — and took prisoners.
21.2: Israel made a solemn pledge to the LORD: 'Give us victory over them, and we will devote every one of their towns to destruction.'
21.3: The LORD heard and gave the Canaanites over. Every person, every town — devoted to destruction. The place was called Hormah: Utter Ruin.
21.4: Setting out from Mount Hor by the Red Sea road to bypass Edom, the people's spirit snapped. The long detour broke them.
21.5: The people turned against God and Moses: 'Why drag us from Egypt into this wilderness to die? No real food, no water — and we are sick of this worthless bread!'
21.6: The LORD sent burning serpents into the camp. They bit the people, and many in Israel died.
21.7: The people came to Moses: 'We have sinned. We spoke against the LORD and against you — pray for us, that he remove the serpents.' Moses prayed on their behalf.
21.8: The LORD gave Moses this remedy: 'Make a bronze snake and lift it on a pole. Let any man who has been bitten look up at it — he will live.' The look of faith, not the ritual object itself, was the cure.
21.9: Moses made a serpent of bronze and raised it on the pole. Whenever someone bitten looked at it, they survived — a sign that would one day point beyond itself to a greater lifting up.
21.10: Israel pressed on and camped at Oboth.
21.11: From Oboth they pressed on to Iye-abarim, in the wilderness east of Moab.
21.12: They moved again and camped in the Wadi Zered.
21.13: From there they crossed to the far bank of the Arnon, the river that cuts through the desert along the Amorite frontier. The Arnon was Moab's northern border.
21.14: An ancient battle-song in the Book of the Wars of the LORD records it: 'The action at Waheb in Suphah, the wadis of the Arnon —'
21.15: 'the gorges falling toward Ar, lying along Moab's boundary.' So the ancient song records the boundary.
21.16: From there to Beer — the Well. It was here the LORD told Moses: 'Gather the people and I will give them water.'
21.17:
Then Israel sang:
'Spring up, O well — sing to it!'
21.18:
'A well the princes dug,
the people's nobles sank it —
with the scepter, with their own staffs.'
From the wilderness they journeyed on to Mattanah,
21.20: to the valley in Moab below Pisgah's summit, looking out over the desert.
21.21: Israel sent an embassy to Sihon, king of the Amorites:
21.22: 'Grant us passage through your land. We will not touch your fields or vineyards or draw from your wells — only the King's Highway, straight through.'
21.23: Sihon refused. He mobilized his entire force and marched into the wilderness to meet Israel. They clashed at Jahaz.
21.24: Israel cut Sihon down and took his entire territory — from the Arnon River to the Jabbok, as far as the Ammonite border, which stopped their advance.
21.25: Every Amorite city fell to Israel. They settled Heshbon and all the villages around it.
21.26: Heshbon was Sihon's capital. He had seized it — and all Moabite territory north to the Arnon — from Moab's former king.
21.27:
The old war-singers put it this way:
'Come to Heshbon! Let it be rebuilt!
Let Sihon's city be restored!'
21.28:
'But fire roared out from Heshbon,
flames from Sihon's own city —
scorching Ar of Moab,
consuming the hilltop lords of the Arnon.'
21.29:
'Ruin on you, Moab!
You are destroyed, people of Chemosh!
Your god surrendered sons to flight,
daughters into captivity —
into the hands of Sihon, Amorite king.'
21.30:
'We have shot them down!
Heshbon in ruins all the way to Dibon,
laid waste to Nophah,
the destruction reaching Medeba.'
21.31: And Israel made the Amorites' land its own.
21.32: Moses sent scouts to Jazer. They took the surrounding villages and expelled the Amorites.
21.33: They pivoted north toward Bashan. Og — its king — marched out with his entire army to engage them at Edrei.
21.34: 'Do not be afraid of him,' the LORD told Moses. 'I am handing him over to you — Og, his army, his entire territory. Deal with him exactly as you dealt with Sihon of Heshbon.'
21.35: Israel struck Og down — sons, army, every last man. No one was left. They took the land.

22.1: Israel moved on and made camp on the plains of Moab, across the Jordan from Jericho.
22.2: Balak son of Zippor had watched Israel dismantle the Amorites.
22.3: The sheer size of Israel's force filled Moab with dread.
22.4: Moab's leaders told the Midianite elders: 'This horde will strip us bare like an ox grazing through a field.' Balak son of Zippor was Moab's king then.
22.5: He dispatched messengers to Balaam son of Beor, a diviner at Pethor by the great river in his homeland: 'A people has come up out of Egypt — they cover the whole face of the land. They are camped at my doorstep.'
22.6: 'Come and curse them for me — they are too strong for me to fight alone. With your curse I might drive them out. I know your power: your blessing holds, and your curse lands.'
22.7: Moab's and Midian's elders set out, carrying Balak's payment for divination. They arrived at Balaam's house and delivered the king's proposal.
22.8: 'Stay the night,' Balaam told them. 'I will bring you the LORD's answer in the morning.' The Moabite princes lodged with him.
22.9: God came to Balaam that night: 'Who are these men staying with you?'
22.10: 'Balak son of Zippor, king of Moab, has sent them,' Balaam answered.
22.11: 'He says a people came from Egypt and has covered the whole land. He wants me to curse them so he can fight them and expel them.'
22.12: 'Do not go with them,' God said. 'You will not curse this people — they are blessed.'
22.13: At daybreak Balaam turned the delegation away: 'Return to your own country. The LORD will not permit me to go with you.'
22.14: The delegation went back to Balak: 'Balaam would not come.'
22.15: Balak sent a second delegation — larger, more prestigious than the first.
22.16: They delivered Balak's message to Balaam: 'King Balak will not take no for an answer. Nothing must prevent you from coming.'
22.17: 'The king will honor you beyond measure — name your terms. Only come and curse this people.'
22.18: Balaam was unswayed: 'Tell Balak this: even if he handed me a palace full of silver and gold, I cannot step one inch past the word of the LORD my God — not in any direction.'
22.19: 'But stay another night. I will see if the LORD has anything more to say to me.'
22.20: God came to Balaam again in the night: 'If these men are calling you, go with them. But you will say exactly what I command you to say, and nothing else.'
22.21: In the morning Balaam saddled his donkey and set out with the Moabite delegation.
22.22: But God's anger burned. The angel of the LORD took up a position in the road to block him. Balaam rode his donkey, two servants alongside.
22.23: The donkey saw the angel of the LORD standing in the path, sword drawn, and veered off the road into a field. Balaam beat her to force her back. He saw nothing.
22.24: The angel moved ahead to a vineyard path, stone walls pressing in on both sides.
22.25: The donkey saw the angel again and pressed hard against the wall, crushing Balaam's foot. He beat her again.
22.26: The angel moved a third time — to a place so narrow there was nowhere to go but forward.
22.27: The donkey simply stopped and lay down beneath him. Balaam's rage boiled over; he struck her with his staff.
22.28: The LORD gave the donkey a voice: 'What have I done to you,' she asked Balaam, 'that you have beaten me three times?'
22.29: 'You have humiliated me!' Balaam answered. 'If I had a sword, you would already be dead!' The prophet rages at his donkey, blind to the real threat.
22.30: 'I have carried you faithfully since the day you first owned me,' the donkey replied. 'When have I ever done anything like this?' 'Never,' Balaam admitted.
22.31: Only then did the LORD open Balaam's eyes. He saw what the donkey had seen all along: the angel of the LORD blocking the road, sword drawn. Balaam threw himself down on his face.
22.32: 'Why have you beaten your donkey three times?' the angel demanded. 'I came out to block you. The path you are taking is leading you to ruin.'
22.33: 'Your donkey saw me each time and turned aside. Had she not turned away, I would have killed you. I would have let her live.' The irony is complete: the animal was wiser than the prophet.
22.34: 'I have sinned,' Balaam said. 'I did not see you there. If this displeases you, I will turn around.'
22.35: 'Continue with them,' the angel said, 'but your mouth will speak only what I give you.' Balaam went on with the Moabite nobles.
22.36: Hearing that Balaam was on his way, Balak rode out to meet him at a frontier city on the Arnon, at the very edge of his territory.
22.37: 'I sent for you twice!' Balak said. 'Why did you not come the first time? Do you think I cannot reward you?'
22.38: 'I am here,' Balaam answered, 'but hear me clearly: I have no power to say whatever I please. I will speak whatever God places in my mouth, and only that.'
22.40: Balak offered cattle and sheep, distributing portions to Balaam and the nobles of the delegation.
22.41: At dawn Balak brought Balaam to the high place of Baal, where he could survey the outer edge of Israel's camp.

23.1: Balaam gave Balak his instructions: 'Build seven altars here and bring seven bulls and seven rams.'
23.2: Balak complied. Together they offered a bull and a ram on each of the seven altars.
23.3: 'Stay here by your sacrifice,' Balaam told Balak. 'I will go seek a meeting with the LORD. Whatever he shows me, I will report.' He went alone to a bare hilltop.
23.4: God met him there. Balaam reported his ritual: 'I have prepared seven altars — a bull and a ram on each.'
23.5: The LORD gave Balaam the words and sent him back: 'Return to Balak and speak this.'
23.6: Balaam returned to find Balak waiting at the sacrifice, the Moabite nobles arrayed around him.
23.7:
Balaam took up his prophetic discourse:
'Balak king of Moab brought me from Aram,
from the mountains of the east —
"Come, curse Jacob for me;
come, denounce Israel."'
23.8:
'How shall I curse what God has not cursed?
How shall I denounce what the LORD has not denounced?'
23.9:
'From rocky heights I look down,
from the hills I survey them:
A people that lives apart,
not reckoned among the nations —
singular, set aside.'
23.10:
'Who can count the dust of Jacob —
number the myriads of Israel?
Let me die as the righteous die!
Let my end be like theirs!'
23.11: 'What have you done?' Balak demanded. 'I brought you here to curse my enemies, and you have blessed them wholesale!'
23.12: 'Am I not bound to speak exactly what the LORD places in my mouth?' Balaam replied.
23.13: 'Come to a different vantage point,' Balak urged. 'From there you will see only the edge of their camp, not the full host. Perhaps a different view will allow you to curse them.'
23.14: Balak led him to the lookout field on Pisgah's summit. Again seven altars; again a bull and a ram on each.
23.15: 'Remain here by the sacrifice,' Balaam told Balak. 'I will meet the LORD over there.'
23.16: The LORD met him and placed words in his mouth: 'Return to Balak and say this.'
23.17: Balak stood at the altar, princes beside him. 'What has the LORD said?' he asked.
23.18:
Balaam began his second oracle:
'Rise up, Balak — listen!
Son of Zippor, attend to me!'
23.19:
'God is not human — he does not lie.
He is not mortal — he does not change his mind.
Has he ever spoken without acting?
Has he ever promised without delivering?'
23.20:
'I received the command to bless.
He has blessed.
I cannot undo it.'
23.21:
'God looks on Jacob and sees no iniquity;
he surveys Israel and finds no perverseness.
The LORD their God is with them,
and the shout of a king rises from their camp.'
23.22:
'God brought them out of Egypt —
he acts with the strength of a wild ox.'
23.23:
'No curse holds against Jacob;
no divination has power over Israel.
In its own time this will be said of Jacob and Israel:
Look what God has done!'
23.24:
'They rise like a lioness aroused for the hunt,
like a lion that will not lie down until the kill is made —
until it has eaten and drunk the blood of the slain.'
23.25: 'Then say nothing at all!' Balak snapped. 'No curse — but no blessing either!'
23.26: 'Did I not warn you from the start?' Balaam answered. 'Whatever the LORD speaks, that is what I will say.'
23.27: 'One more try,' Balak insisted. 'Perhaps God will be pleased with a different spot and you will be able to curse them from there.'
23.28: Balak brought him to the summit of Peor, overlooking the desert.
23.29: Same ritual again: 'Build seven altars,' Balaam said, 'and bring seven bulls and seven rams.'
23.30: Balak obeyed, offering a bull and a ram on each of the seven altars.

24.1: Balaam understood now: the LORD had determined to bless Israel. He abandoned divination altogether and turned to face the open desert.
24.2: He saw Israel spread out in orderly tribal camps, and the Spirit of God came upon him.
24.3:
His third oracle:
'The word of Balaam son of Beor,
the word of the man whose eyes are opened —'
24.4:
'who hears the words of God,
who sees what the Almighty shows,
who falls in a trance, yet sees with open eyes.'
24.5:
'How beautiful your tents, O Jacob!
Your encampments, O Israel!'
24.6:
'Like broad valleys, like gardens along a river,
like aloe trees the LORD himself has planted,
like cedar trees beside living water.'
24.7:
'Water overflows his pails —
his seed grows in abundant streams.
His king will rise greater than Agag;
his kingdom will be lifted up.'
24.8:
'God brought him out of Egypt —
powerful as a wild ox.
He will consume the nations that oppose him,
crush their bones,
pierce them through with his arrows.'
24.9:
'He crouches, lies down like a lion,
like the king of beasts —
who would dare to rouse him?
Blessed are those who bless you;
cursed are those who curse you.'
24.10: Balak erupted in fury. He clapped his hands together: 'I brought you here to curse my enemies, and you have blessed them three times running!'
24.11: 'Go home! I intended to make you rich and famous, but the LORD has denied you that reward.'
24.12: 'Did I not tell your very first delegation,' Balaam replied to Balak,'
24.13: 'Even a palace full of silver and gold could not move me past the LORD's command. I have no will in this matter. What the LORD says is what comes out of my mouth.'
24.14: 'I am returning to my own people. But hear this first — what Israel will do to Moab in the days to come.'
24.15:
He began a fourth oracle:
'The word of Balaam son of Beor,
the word of the man whose eyes are opened —'
24.16:
'who hears the words of God,
who knows what the Most High knows,
who sees what the Almighty shows —
falling in a trance, yet seeing with open eyes.'
24.18:
'Edom will fall before him — Seir too, his enemies both.
Israel will rise to greatness.'
24.19:
'Out of Jacob will come the ruler
who sweeps away what remains of every city.'
24.20:
Balaam turned his gaze toward Amalek and spoke:
'Amalek rose first among the nations —
but their end is eternal ruin.'
24.21:
He looked at the Kenites:
'Your home is stronghold,
your nest built in the rock —'
24.22:
'— yet even Kain will be ruined;
Asshur will carry you into exile.'
24.23: 'Alas — who survives when God unleashes this?'
24.24:
'Fleets from Kittim will come,
and they will humble Asshur, humble Eber —
but they in turn will also perish.'
24.25: With that, Balaam set out on the road back to his homeland. Balak went his own way.

13.2: Send scouts into Canaan — the land I am giving to Israel. One man from each tribe, a recognized leader whose report will carry weight.
13.3: Moses dispatched them from the Paran wilderness at the LORD's command. Every one was a man of standing in Israel.
13.4: Their names: from Reuben — Shammua son of Zaccur.
13.5: From Simeon — Shaphat son of Hori.
13.6: From Judah — Caleb son of Jephunneh.
13.7: From Issachar — Igal son of Joseph.
13.8: From Ephraim — Hoshea son of Nun.
13.9: From Benjamin — Palti son of Raphu.
13.10: From Zebulun — Gaddiel son of Sodi.
13.11: From Joseph, specifically from Manasseh — Gaddi son of Susi.
13.12: From Dan — Ammiel son of Gemalli.
13.13: From Asher — Sethur son of Michael.
13.14: From Naphtali — Nahbi son of Vophsi.
13.15: From Gad — Geuel son of Machi.
13.16: These were the twelve scouts Moses sent into Canaan. He also renamed Hoshea son of Nun: Joshua — 'the LORD saves.'
13.17: Moses gave them their orders: 'Go up through the Negeb and into the hill country.'
13.18: Assess everything: the land itself, its people — strong or weak, few or many —
13.19: — whether the land is good or poor, whether the settlements are open villages or walled fortresses —
13.20: — whether the soil is rich or thin, whether there is timber. Be bold — and bring back fruit from the land.' It was the season of the first ripe grapes.
13.21: The twelve scouts swept the entire length of the land — from the Zin wilderness in the south all the way north to Rehob near Lebo-hamath.
13.22: Pushing into the Negeb, they reached Hebron — an ancient city, already standing seven years before Egypt's Zoan was built. There they encountered Ahiman, Sheshai, and Talmai, of Anak's clan.
13.23: At the valley of Eshcol they found grapes so heavy that one cluster required two men and a carrying-pole. They also brought pomegranates and figs — the land's abundance made tangible.
13.24: They named the valley Eshcol — Cluster — for that one bunch of grapes that took two men to carry.
13.25: After forty days in the land, the scouts came home.
13.26: They returned to Kadesh in the Paran wilderness and reported to Moses, Aaron, and the whole congregation, setting the land's fruit before everyone.
13.27: 'We went to the land you sent us to — it is everything you said. Milk and honey. Here is the proof.' They held up the grapes.
13.28: But — the people are formidable. Their cities are massive and walled. And we saw Anak's descendants there.
13.29: The Amalekites hold the Negeb. The Hittites, Jebusites, and Amorites occupy the hills. The Canaanites hold the coast and the Jordan valley. The land is fully garrisoned.
13.30: Caleb cut through the noise: 'We should go up now and take it. We can do this.'
13.31: The other scouts pushed back: 'We cannot attack those people. They are stronger than we are.'
13.32: They spread a slanderous report through Israel: 'That land eats its own people. Every person we saw was enormous.'
13.33: We saw the Nephilim — ancient warrior-giants, Anak's line. Standing next to them we felt like grasshoppers. And that is exactly how we looked to them.

14.1: The whole camp broke into weeping. The cry rose through the night.
14.2: 'Why didn't we die in Egypt? Why not here in the wilderness?' The entire congregation turned on Moses and Aaron.
14.3: 'The LORD is leading us to our deaths. Our families will be taken as plunder. Egypt is better than this.'
14.4: They began electing an alternative leader among themselves: 'We are going back to Egypt.'
14.5: Moses and Aaron fell facedown before the whole congregation — an act of intercession, not defeat.
14.6: Joshua son of Nun and Caleb son of Jephunneh — the two faithful scouts — tore their garments in grief and protest.
14.7: They cried out to the assembly: 'The land is extraordinarily good. We saw it with our own eyes.'
14.8: 'If the LORD is with us — and he is — he will bring us into it and give it to us. A land of milk and honey. It is ours to receive.'
14.9: 'Do not rebel against the LORD. Do not be afraid of those people — they are food for us. Their protection has been stripped away. The LORD is with us. Fear nothing.'
14.10: The crowd called for their execution. Then the glory of the LORD blazed out at the tent of meeting — seen by all Israel.
14.11: The LORD said to Moses: 'How long will this people treat me with contempt? How long before they trust me, after everything they have seen me do?'
14.12: 'I will wipe them out with plague and disown them. From you alone I will build a greater nation.'
14.13: Moses appealed: 'Wait. Egypt will hear what happened. You brought this people out of Egypt with visible, undeniable power —'
14.14: '— and Canaan's peoples already know. They know you are visibly among Israel: your cloud overhead, your pillar of fire ahead. You have been seen face to face among your people.'
14.15: 'If you destroy all Israel at a stroke, every nation that has heard your name will draw one conclusion:'
14.16: 'He could not deliver what he promised. He abandoned them in the wilderness.' Your name will be mocked among the nations.
14.17: Moses pressed on: 'Now let your power be shown as great — shown in exactly the way you yourself declared at Sinai:'
14.18: 'The LORD is slow to anger and rich in covenant love — forgiving iniquity and rebellion, yet never leaving guilt unaddressed, its weight passing through three and four generations.' Moses quoted the Sinai covenant formula back to God.
14.19: 'Pardon their iniquity — let your covenant love be equal to the moment, as it has been from Egypt to this day.'
14.20: The LORD answered: 'I have pardoned them, as you asked.'
14.21: 'But hear this: as truly as I live, the whole earth will one day be filled with my glory —'
14.22: '— none of those who saw my glory in Egypt, who witnessed my signs in the wilderness, and who have tested me ten times now, defying my voice each time —'
14.23: '— not one of those contemptuous people will set foot in the land I swore to their ancestors. They will not see it.'
14.24: 'Caleb is different. He has a different spirit — one of trust and full allegiance. He followed me without reservation. I will bring him into the very land he scouted, and his descendants will inherit it.'
14.25: The Amalekites and Canaanites hold the valleys — the direct route to Canaan is blocked. Turn back; head toward the wilderness and the Red Sea.
14.26: The LORD spoke to Moses and Aaron:
14.27: 'How long must I endure this? This community's grumbling has reached me again — aimed directly against me.'
14.28: 'Tell them: as I live — the LORD's own word — I will give them precisely what they demanded in my hearing.'
14.29: 'Every person twenty and older who was registered and who grumbled against me — their bodies will lie in this wilderness.'
14.30: Not one of them will enter the promised land — except Caleb son of Jephunneh and Joshua son of Nun.
14.31: 'The children you said would become plunder — they will enter. They will know and inhabit the land their parents despised.'
14.32: The parents: their bones will bleach in this wilderness.
14.33: Their children will wander forty years, bearing the consequence of their parents' infidelity — until the last faithless body is gone.
14.34: Forty days of scouting: forty years of consequence. One year per day. They will learn, at length, what it costs to oppose the LORD.
14.35: The LORD has spoken it and will not retract it: every person in this rebellious community who united against him — they will be worn down to nothing in this wilderness. Here they will die.
14.36: The scouts who brought back the slanderous report — who ignited the whole congregation's rebellion against Moses —
14.37: — were struck by plague and died before the LORD. The punishment was immediate.
14.38: Only Joshua and Caleb survived among the scouts — the two who had refused to lie.
14.39: When Moses delivered the LORD's verdict to Israel, the grief was overwhelming.
14.40: By morning they had reversed course: 'We were wrong. We will go up to the land the LORD promised.' They started climbing toward Canaan.
14.41: Moses warned them: 'Why do you transgress the LORD's command now? It is too late. This will not succeed.'
14.42: 'Do not go. The LORD is not with you. You will be cut down by your enemies.'
14.43: 'The Amalekites and Canaanites are waiting ahead of you. You have turned away from the LORD, and the LORD has withdrawn from you. The sword is what you will meet.'
14.44: They went anyway — presumptuously, without the ark, without Moses, without the LORD. They marched toward disaster.
14.45: The Amalekites and Canaanites swarmed down and crushed them — routing Israel all the way to Hormah.

15.2: Tell the Israelites: When you enter the land I am giving you as your home —
15.3: when you offer a fire offering to the LORD — burnt offering, vow fulfillment, freewill offering, or festival sacrifice — from herd or flock, a pleasing aroma before the LORD —
15.4: — the accompanying grain offering is one-tenth of an ephah of fine flour mixed with a quarter hin of oil.
15.5: Add a quarter hin of wine as a drink offering alongside each lamb.
15.6: For a ram: two-tenths of an ephah of fine flour, one-third hin of oil —
15.7: — and a third hin of wine as a drink offering, a pleasing aroma to the LORD.
15.8: When the offering is a bull — burnt offering, vow fulfillment, or fellowship offering —
15.9: — bring three-tenths of an ephah of fine flour mixed with half a hin of oil,
15.10: — and half a hin of wine as a drink offering. A pleasing aroma before the LORD.
15.11: These proportions apply to each animal — bull, ram, lamb, or goat.
15.12: Multiply the proportions by the number of animals. Every offering receives its full accompaniment.
15.13: Every native-born Israelite who brings a fire offering to the LORD follows these rules.
15.14: If a foreigner living among you — in any generation — brings a fire offering to the LORD, the same procedure applies.
15.15: One law, one standard — for the native-born and the resident alien alike, permanent through every generation. Before the LORD, the foreigner stands equal to the native Israelite.
15.16: One law. One ordinance. No distinction at the altar.
15.18: Tell the Israelites: When you enter the land I am bringing you into,
15.19: when you eat the land's grain, set aside a contribution for the LORD.
15.20: From the first batch of dough, bring a loaf as a contribution — the same principle as the threshing-floor firstfruits.
15.21: In every generation: the first bread belongs to the LORD.
15.22: If the community sins unintentionally — failing to observe some commandment the LORD gave through Moses —
15.23: — any of the LORD's commands given through Moses, from the beginning onward through every generation —
15.24: if this happened without the community realizing it, the whole congregation shall offer one bull as a burnt offering with its grain and drink offering, and one male goat as a sin offering. The community bears the unintentional mistake together.
15.25: The priest makes atonement for the whole community and they receive forgiveness — because it was unintentional. The sacrifice covers the community's accidental failure.
15.26: The entire community is forgiven — native Israelite and resident foreigner alike. When the failure was genuinely unintentional, the atonement covers everyone.
15.27: If an individual sins unintentionally, they bring a yearling female goat as a sin offering.
15.28: The priest makes atonement for the individual and they receive forgiveness. The provision for unintentional sin exists precisely to be used.
15.29: One provision covers everyone: the same law for the unintentional failure of the native-born and the sojourner alike.
15.30: But deliberate defiance is different. Anyone — Israelite or foreigner — who acts with a raised fist against the LORD treats him with contempt. That person is cut off from Israel.
15.31: They despised the LORD's word and broke his commandment deliberately. Cut off completely. The guilt stays with them.
15.32: While Israel was still in the wilderness, a man was found gathering firewood on the Sabbath.
15.33: He was brought before Moses, Aaron, and the whole community.
15.34: They held him in custody while waiting for the LORD's ruling — the specific penalty had not yet been stated.
15.35: The LORD ruled: death. The whole community will stone him outside the camp.
15.36: The community carried out the sentence outside the camp. The Sabbath boundary was absolute, and the cost of treating it otherwise was absolute.
15.38: Tell Israel: sew tassels on the corners of every garment — in every generation — and thread a blue cord through each tassel.
15.39: You will see the tassels and remember every commandment of the LORD — and keep them. They are a check on the heart and the eyes, which tend to chase after what is forbidden.
15.40: Remembering leads to obedience; obedience leads to holiness — being set apart for the LORD your God.
15.41: I am the LORD your God — the one who brought you out of Egypt to be your God. I am the LORD your God.

16.1: Korah son of Izhar, a Kohathite Levite, joined with Dathan and Abiram sons of Eliab, and On son of Peleth — Reubenites — and mounted a challenge.
16.2: With them came 250 leading men of Israel — established names, known voices in the assembly. Together they confronted Moses.
16.3: 'You have overstepped,' they charged. 'Every person in this congregation is holy. The LORD is among all of us equally. Who made you leaders over the LORD's people?'
16.4: Moses's response to the challenge: he fell on his face before the LORD.
16.5: He said to Korah and his company: 'Wait until morning. The LORD himself will show who belongs to him, who is truly holy, who he has chosen. He will draw that person near.'
16.6: Moses set the terms of the test: 'You want the priesthood? Very well — take your incense pans, Korah and all your company.'
16.7: 'Stand before the LORD tomorrow with fire and incense. The man the LORD chooses as holy — that will settle who belongs at the altar. It is you Levites who have gone too far.'
16.8: Then Moses spoke directly to Korah: 'Sons of Levi — listen carefully.'
16.9: 'Is what you already have not enough? The God of Israel separated you from all Israel and brought you near to himself — to serve at his tabernacle, to stand before the whole community on their behalf. That is an extraordinary honor.'
16.10: 'He brought you near — all of you, the entire Levite tribe. And now you want the priesthood as well? Where does the ambition end?'
16.11: 'Your quarrel is not with Aaron. It is with the LORD who chose him. Aaron is nothing by himself — your anger is aimed at God.'
16.12: Moses sent for Dathan and Abiram. Their answer: 'We refuse to come.'
16.13: 'Was it not enough to drag us out of Egypt — the land of milk and honey — to die in this wilderness? Now you want to rule over us as well?'
16.14: 'You have not delivered a promised land. No fields, no vineyards. Do you think you can deceive us forever? We are not coming.'
16.15: Moses was furious. He prayed: 'LORD, do not accept their offering. I have not taken so much as a donkey from any of them. I have not wronged a single person.'
16.16: Moses addressed Korah: 'Tomorrow. You, your whole company, and Aaron — all before the LORD.'
16.17: 'Each man brings his own incense pan with incense burning — 250 pans, plus yours and Aaron's. Every man presents himself, and the LORD decides.'
16.18: The 250 men each lit their censers and took their place at the entrance of the tent of meeting with Moses and Aaron. The test was set.
16.19: Korah assembled the whole congregation at the tent of meeting entrance — as if numbers would sway the verdict. Then the glory of the LORD blazed out before all of them.
16.20: The LORD spoke to Moses and Aaron:
16.21: 'Step away from this congregation. I am about to consume them in a moment.'
16.22: Moses and Aaron fell facedown and prayed: 'O God — God who holds the life-breath of every person — when one man sins, must the whole congregation bear it?'
16.24: 'Warn the congregation: clear away from the tents of Korah, Dathan, and Abiram.'
16.25: Moses went straight to Dathan and Abiram, the elders of Israel walking with him.
16.26: He called to the congregation: 'Get away from these men's tents. Touch nothing of theirs. If you stay near, their punishment may sweep you away with them.'
16.27: The congregation drew back on all sides. Dathan and Abiram stepped out and stood defiantly at their tent doors — their wives, sons, and small children gathered around them.
16.28: Moses addressed all Israel: 'This is how you will know the LORD sent me — and that none of this has been my own idea:'
16.29: 'If these men die ordinary deaths — the kind every human being dies — then the LORD did not send me.'
16.30: 'But if the LORD does something unprecedented — if the ground opens and swallows them alive, descending to Sheol with everything that is theirs — then you will know for certain these men have defied the LORD.'
16.31: The moment Moses finished speaking, the ground split open beneath them.
16.32: The earth swallowed them — tents, households, every person and possession attached to Korah.
16.33: They descended alive into Sheol. The ground sealed over them. They were excised from Israel in an instant.
16.34: Everyone nearby ran at the sound of their screaming — the terrifying thought in every mind: 'We could be next.'
16.35: Then fire came out from the LORD and burned up the 250 men who had stood at the entrance with their censers.
16.37: Tell Eleazar son of Aaron to gather the censers from among the charred remains and scatter the coals away. The censers themselves are holy —
16.38: — even these sinners' censers carry consecration, having been lifted before the LORD. Hammer them into altar plating: a permanent warning to every generation of what happens when unauthorized hands reach for the priesthood.
16.39: Eleazar collected the bronze censers from the incinerated men and had them beaten flat as altar plating —
16.40: — a permanent visual warning embedded in the altar itself: no outsider, no one not of Aaron's descent, approaches the incense altar. The plating said without words: this is what happened to Korah.
16.41: The very next morning, the whole congregation turned on Moses and Aaron: 'You killed the LORD's people.'
16.42: As the mob formed against Moses and Aaron, they looked toward the tent of meeting — and the cloud had already covered it. The glory of the LORD was visibly present.
16.43: Moses and Aaron went forward and stood before the tent of meeting.
16.45: 'Move away from this congregation — I am about to destroy them instantly.' Moses and Aaron fell facedown.
16.46: Moses turned to Aaron: 'Take your censer. Fire from the altar, incense on it — run to the people and make atonement. The LORD's wrath has gone out — the plague has already started.'
16.47: Aaron ran into the thick of the congregation with his censer smoking. The plague was already spreading through the camp. He stood between the living and the dead and made atonement.
16.48: He stood at the boundary between death and life, and the plague stopped.
16.49: Fourteen thousand seven hundred died in the plague — apart from those who had died with Korah.
16.50: Aaron returned to Moses at the tent of meeting. The plague was over.

17.2: Collect from Israel's leaders one staff per ancestral house — twelve staffs in all. Write each leader's name on his staff.
17.3: Write Aaron's name on Levi's staff — one staff, one tribe, one name represents the house.
17.4: Place all the staffs before the covenant tablets in the tent of meeting — where I meet with you.
17.5: The staff of the man I have chosen will blossom. This will settle, once and for all, who is chosen — and silence Israel's endless grumbling.
17.6: Moses relayed the command and collected the staffs — one from each leader, twelve in all, Aaron's among them.
17.7: Moses laid all twelve staffs before the LORD in the tent of meeting.
17.8: By the next morning, Aaron's staff had burst into life: buds, blossoms, and ripe almonds — all overnight, without soil or water. It was unmistakable.
17.9: Moses brought out the twelve staffs and showed them to all Israel. Eleven were unchanged. Aaron's alone had bloomed.
17.10: The LORD told Moses: 'Keep Aaron's staff in front of the covenant tablets — a permanent sign against rebellion. Let it end the grumbling before the grumbling ends them.'
17.12: Israel cried out to Moses: 'We are dying! We are ruined! Every approach to the holy place kills us!'
17.13: 'Anyone who draws near the tabernacle dies. Is there no end to the dying?'

18.1: The LORD told Aaron: 'You and your sons and your father's house — you carry the consequences of any breach at the sanctuary. You and your sons bear the weight of the priesthood's failures.'
18.2: Bring your fellow Levites — your kinsmen by birth — to serve alongside you as assistants. You and your sons remain responsible before the tent of meeting.
18.3: The Levites serve the tabernacle under your oversight — but they must not touch the sacred furnishings or the altar. That boundary is the difference between life and death.
18.4: They join you in maintaining the tent of meeting. No unauthorized person may approach you.
18.5: You and your sons guard the altar and sanctuary. Your faithfulness is Israel's protection against divine wrath.
18.6: I have given you the Levites — drawn from Israel as a gift to me, now given to you — to serve at the tent of meeting under your oversight.
18.7: You and your sons alone attend to the priestly work — at the altar and within the curtain before the Most Holy Place. I give you this priesthood as a gift. Anyone else who approaches dies.
18.8: The LORD said to Aaron: 'I give you charge of all the contributions brought to me — the holy gifts of Israel. They belong to you and your sons as your allotted portion — a permanent provision.'
18.9: From the most holy offerings — what is not consumed by fire — every grain offering, sin offering, and guilt offering brought to me as most holy belongs to you and your sons.
18.10: You eat it in the most holy place — only the males of your household. It is most holy.
18.11: Also yours: the wave offerings and contributions Israel brings — for you, your sons, and daughters. Every clean person in your household may share in this.
18.12: The finest of the oil, the finest wine and grain — the very best of Israel's firstfruits — these belong to you.
18.13: The first ripe fruits of the land brought to the LORD are yours. All clean members of your household may eat.
18.14: Everything in Israel placed under the ban — devoted entirely to the LORD — becomes yours.
18.15: Every firstborn that opens the womb — human or animal — brought to the LORD is yours. But every firstborn human must be redeemed, and every unclean animal's firstborn must be redeemed.
18.16: The redemption price, paid from one month old onward: five sanctuary shekels — twenty gerahs each.
18.17: The firstborn of ox, sheep, or goat cannot be redeemed — they are holy to the LORD. Their blood is sprinkled on the altar, their fat burned as a pleasing offering.
18.18: The meat is yours — like the wave-offering breast and the right thigh that always belong to the priest.
18.19: All the holy contributions Israel brings to the LORD — given to you, your sons and daughters, as a lasting covenant. A covenant of salt: enduring, binding, preserved against decay, unbreakable before the LORD forever.
18.20: The LORD said to Aaron: 'You will own no land. You receive no territorial allotment. I am your inheritance — I myself, among all Israel.'
18.21: I give all of Israel's tithes to the Levites as their inheritance — payment for their service at the tent of meeting.
18.22: Israel at large must not approach the tent of meeting. To cross that boundary is to risk death.
18.23: The Levites serve at the tent of meeting and bear the weight of any breach there. And their portion is not land — in every generation, they receive no territorial inheritance among Israel.
18.24: The Israelites' tithe — which they lift to the LORD as an offering — goes to the Levites. That is their inheritance. Therefore they receive no land.
18.26: Tell the Levites: When you receive the tithe from Israel as your inheritance, you in turn are to present a tithe of that tithe to the LORD.
18.27: This tithe-of-a-tithe you present will be counted for you exactly as if it were firstfruits from the threshing floor or the winepress.
18.28: From everything the Levites receive from Israel, they present a portion to the LORD — and that portion goes to Aaron.
18.29: Give the LORD's portion from the best of what you receive — the holiest, finest part.
18.30: Once you have lifted the best portion to the LORD, the remainder counts for you just as the farmer's harvest counts — yours to enjoy.
18.31: You and your families may eat the rest freely, anywhere you choose — it is your earned wage for service at the tent of meeting.
18.32: No guilt attaches to eating the rest, once you have given the LORD's portion from the best. But handle the holy gifts carelessly and it will cost your life.

25.1: At Shittim — with the promised land in sight across the Jordan — Israel fell into sexual immorality with the women of Moab.
25.2: Table fellowship led to worship. The women invited Israel to their sacrificial feasts — and Israel ate at pagan altars and bowed before pagan gods.
25.3: Israel had yoked itself to Baal of Peor — a rival god, a dead end. The LORD's anger ignited.
25.4: The LORD told Moses: execute the guilty leaders publicly — exposed before the LORD in broad daylight — so my burning wrath against Israel may subside.
25.5: Moses told the tribal judges: you are each responsible for those under your authority. Execute every man who joined himself to Baal of Peor.
25.6: At the very moment the nation was weeping before the tent of meeting, an Israelite man walked up and brazenly escorted a Midianite woman into the camp — in full view of Moses and all Israel.
25.7: Phinehas — grandson of Aaron, son of the high priest — saw what was happening. He rose from the weeping congregation, seized a spear,
25.8: and followed the man into the inner room. One thrust killed them both. The plague stopped.
25.9: Twenty-four thousand were dead — the plague's toll on a single episode of faithlessness.
25.11: Phinehas matched my own jealous fire with his — his zeal for my honor deflected my wrath. Had he not acted, my jealousy would have consumed Israel entirely.
25.12: Therefore tell him: I grant him my covenant of peace —
25.13: — a covenant of everlasting priesthood for him and his line. Because he burned with zeal for his God and made atonement for Israel, the priesthood is secured through him.
25.14: The Israelite: Zimri son of Salu — not an anonymous fool but a tribal leader, a prince of Simeon's ancestral house.
25.15: The woman: Cozbi daughter of Zur — herself the daughter of a Midianite tribal chieftain. This was a deliberate provocation, not a naive entanglement.
25.17: Make war on the Midianites. Strike them.
25.18: Midian engineered this. They used Cozbi as a weapon — deploying the daughter of their own chief as a deliberate snare. The plague was the consequence; Midian was the cause.

26.1: The plague was over. A generation had been judged. The LORD now spoke to Moses and Eleazar:
26.2: Count the new generation: every Israelite male from twenty years old — the age of military service — enrolled by ancestral house.
26.3: Moses and Eleazar issued the command in the plains of Moab, across the Jordan from Jericho — standing at the threshold of the promised land.
26.4: Count from twenty years upward, as the LORD had commanded. These are the children of those who left Egypt, not the exodus generation itself.
26.5: Reuben, firstborn of Israel. His clans: Hanochites from Hanoch, Palluites from Pallu;
26.6: Hezronites from Hezron, Carmites from Carmi.
26.9: Eliab's sons: Nemuel — and Dathan and Abiram, the two rebels who made themselves notorious by challenging Moses, Aaron, and the LORD himself in Korah's revolt.
26.10: The earth swallowed them with Korah; fire consumed the 250. Their fate was inscribed into Israel's collective memory as a permanent warning.
26.11: Korah's own sons, however, survived — their line would go on to compose many of the Psalms.
26.14: Total Simeonites: 22,200 — the smallest tribe in this census.
26.19: Judah's firstborn sons — Er and Onan — both died in Canaan before the exodus. Only their names survive in the genealogy.
26.20: Judah's living clans: Shelanites, Pharzites, Zerahites.
26.22: Total Judah: 76,500 — the largest tribe in the census, consistent with the blessing of Jacob.
26.28: Joseph counts through his two sons — each elevated to a full tribe.
26.29: Manasseh's main line: Machir → Gilead, and from Gilead all the Gileadite clans.
26.33: Hepher's son Zelophehad left only daughters — five of them: Mahlah, Noah, Hoglah, Milcah, and Tirzah. Their names appear here; their case will decide law in the next chapter.
26.37: Total Ephraim: 32,500. Joseph's two tribes together: 85,200.
26.40: Bela's sons Ard and Naaman each founded their own clan: Ardites and Naamites.
26.42: Dan's entire lineage runs through one son: Shuham — a single clan comprising the whole tribe.
26.43: Total Dan: 64,400 — a large tribe from a single ancestral line.
26.46: A daughter of Asher: Serah — named here in a census of sons, a rare inclusion.
26.51: The grand total: 601,730 — a new generation, slightly fewer than the first census (603,550), ready to inherit what their parents forfeited.
26.53: The land will be apportioned to these — each tribe's share determined by the size of its registered population.
26.54: More people, more land; fewer people, less. The allotment is proportionally just.
26.55: But lot, not calculation alone, makes the final assignment — God's hand in the distribution, not merely human arithmetic.
26.56: Lot governs all: God apportions; no one may object.
26.57: The Levites are counted separately. Their three great clans: Gershonites, Kohathites, Merarites.
26.58: Levite sub-clans: Libnites, Hebronites, Mahlites, Mushites, and Korathites — Korah's very line, still present in Israel. Kohath's son was Amram.
26.59: Amram's wife was Jochebed — daughter of Levi himself, born in Egypt. She is the mother of three of the most consequential figures in Israel's history: Aaron, Moses, and Miriam.
26.60: Aaron's four sons: Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar.
26.61: Nadab and Abihu are dead — struck down for offering unauthorized fire before the LORD. Of Aaron's four sons, only Eleazar and Ithamar remain.
26.62: Levites: 23,000 males from a month old — counted separately because they hold no territorial inheritance. The LORD is their inheritance.
26.63: This census — taken by Moses and Eleazar on the plains of Moab — was a complete registration of the new generation.
26.64: Not a single person from this list had appeared in the Sinai census forty years earlier.
26.65: The LORD's word had been kept exactly: everyone from the Sinai generation died in the wilderness. Every one. Only Caleb and Joshua appear in both censuses — the only two who had been faithful.

27.1: Five daughters of Zelophehad — deep in Manasseh's line, their father's name already recorded in the census — stepped forward with a legal claim. Their names: Mahlah, Noah, Hoglah, Milcah, and Tirzah.
27.2: They stood before the full court of Israel — Moses, the high priest, the tribal leaders, all the people — at the entrance to the tent of meeting, and presented their case:
27.3: 'Our father died in the wilderness — but not as a rebel. He was not with Korah. He died for his own sin, as all of that generation did. He simply had no sons.'
27.4: 'Why should his name vanish from his family's inheritance merely because he had daughters instead of sons? Grant us a share among our father's kinsmen.'
27.5: Moses did not rule alone. He took their petition directly to the LORD.
27.7: 'The daughters of Zelophehad are right. Give them an inheritance among their father's brothers — their father's share passes to them. The law changes today.'
27.8: Tell all Israel: when a man dies without a son, his inheritance passes to his daughter.
27.9: No daughter? Then to his brothers.
27.10: No brothers? Then to his father's brothers.
27.11: No father's brothers? Then the nearest kin. This ruling — daughters, brothers, father's brothers, nearest kin — is now permanent law in Israel, decreed by the LORD himself.
27.12: Then the LORD turned to Moses: climb the Abarim range. Look at the land I am giving Israel.
27.13: You will see it — but you will not enter. Like Aaron, you will be gathered to your people after that final view.
27.14: Because at Meribah-Kadesh, when Israel was in rebellion and thirsty, you failed to honor my holiness before them. That single moment of faithlessness stands against even Moses.
27.15: Moses did not protest his own sentence. He prayed for the people:
27.16: 'LORD — you who hold the breath of every living being — appoint someone over this congregation,
27.17: who will march before them and lead them back — so that the LORD's people are not left like sheep with no one to guide them.'
27.18: The LORD answered: take Joshua son of Nun — a man in whom the Spirit already lives — and lay your hand on him.
27.19: Bring him before the high priest and all Israel; commission him publicly so every person witnesses it.
27.20: Give him a portion of your own authority — enough that Israel will follow him as they have followed you.
27.21: Joshua will not have Moses's direct access to God. He will consult through the Urim, mediated by the high priest. But when the priest speaks the LORD's word, all Israel moves and halts on that word.
27.22: Moses obeyed without delay. He brought Joshua before Eleazar and all the assembled people.
27.23: He laid both hands on Joshua and charged him with the task — passing the mantle exactly as the LORD had specified.

28.2: Command Israel: the fire offerings that rise as a pleasing aroma to me must be presented at their appointed times without fail. This is my food — Israel's covenant obligation on the altar.
28.3: The daily offering: two perfect yearling lambs, every single day without exception. This is the foundation on which all other sacrifices rest.
28.4: One at dawn, one at dusk — the day bracketed by sacrifice.
28.5: Alongside the lamb: fine flour with oil — one-tenth ephah, one-quarter hin of pressed oil.
28.6: This offering was established at Sinai — not an addition but the foundational rhythm of Israel's worship from the beginning.
28.7: One-quarter hin of fermented drink poured before the LORD in the sanctuary — wine offered to God in the place of his presence.
28.8: The evening lamb mirrors the morning's exactly — same grain, same drink. The day closes as it opened: with fire and aroma rising before the LORD.
28.9: On the Sabbath: double the daily offering — two more yearling lambs, two-tenths of flour with oil. The Sabbath is marked by abundance, not by reduction.
28.10: Sabbath additions on top of the daily — the regular offering is never suspended.
28.11: At each new moon, a full offering: two bulls, one ram, seven yearling lambs — all unblemished. Each month begins with consecration.
28.12: Grain alongside each animal: three-tenths ephah per bull, two-tenths per ram, all blended with oil.
28.13: One-tenth per lamb. Every portion calculated; nothing arbitrary before the LORD.
28.14: Wine portions: half a hin per bull, a third per ram, a quarter per lamb — these proportions govern every new moon, every month, year round.
28.15: Plus one goat for atonement — every new moon comes with acknowledgment of sin, not only celebration of the month.
28.16: The fourteenth of the first month: Passover — the founding feast of Israel's identity as a redeemed people.
28.17: The fifteenth begins seven days of Unleavened Bread — bread without leaven, remembering the rush of redemption.
28.18: Day one: a sacred assembly, rest from ordinary labor.
28.19: Offerings for each of the seven days: two bulls, one ram, seven yearling lambs — all perfect.
28.20: Grain: three-tenths per bull, two-tenths per ram.
28.22: One goat for atonement — even festival days carry this acknowledgment.
28.23: All of this comes on top of the daily morning sacrifice — the festival does not replace the routine; it adds to it.
28.24: This pattern holds for all seven days of Unleavened Bread — the festive offering alongside the constant daily, holiday and routine woven together.
28.25: The seventh day closes with another sacred assembly — Unleavened Bread ends as it began, with gathered worship.
28.26: Then at Weeks — Shavuot, the day of firstfruits, when the new harvest's grain is first offered — another sacred assembly, another day free from ordinary work.
28.27: Shavuot's burnt offering: two bulls, one ram, seven yearling lambs.
28.28: Grain: three-tenths per bull, two-tenths per ram.
28.30: One goat for atonement — the harvest feast is not complete without it.
28.31: All of this alongside the regular daily sacrifice — every animal without blemish, every portion with its drink offering. This is Israel's covenant calendar through the first half of the year.

29.1: The first of Tishri: a sacred assembly, no labor, a day of trumpet blasts — the origin of what becomes Rosh Hashanah, the new year's awakening.
29.2: One bull, one ram, seven yearling lambs — the offering sounding with the trumpets.
29.3: Grain: three-tenths for the bull, two-tenths for the ram.
29.4: One-tenth for each of the seven lambs.
29.5: One goat for atonement — even the new year's trumpet day carries the weight of sin.
29.6: On top of the new moon offering and the daily — the Day of Trumpets adds its own layer. Three streams of sacrifice rising together.
29.7: The tenth of Tishri: the Day of Atonement — a sacred assembly, full fasting, complete rest. This is the most solemn day in Israel's entire calendar.
29.8: Yom Kippur's burnt offering: one bull, one ram, seven yearling lambs — all perfect. While the nation fasts, the altar is full.
29.9: Grain: three-tenths per bull, two-tenths per ram.
29.11: Plus a sin-offering goat — alongside the special Yom Kippur rites from Leviticus 16, and on top of the daily. Atonement Day is saturated with sacrifice.
29.12: The fifteenth of Tishri: Tabernacles begins — seven days of feasting before the LORD. After the solemnity of Atonement comes the great joy of the harvest feast.
29.13: Tabernacles Day 1: thirteen bulls, two rams, fourteen yearling lambs. The thirteen bulls will count down to seven over the week — a pattern unique in Israel's calendar.
29.14: Grain: three-tenths per bull (all thirteen), two-tenths per ram.
29.16: One goat for atonement — Day 1, alongside the perpetual daily.
29.17: Day 2: twelve bulls — one fewer than yesterday. Two rams and fourteen lambs remain constant throughout.
29.18: Grain and drink as prescribed, proportioned to each animal's count.
29.20: Day 3: eleven bulls. The count descends by one each day.
29.21: Grain and drink proportioned to each animal.
29.32: Day 7: seven bulls — the count reaches its floor. Over seven days, 70 bulls have been offered in total.
29.33: Grain and drink as prescribed for the seventh day.
29.34: One goat for atonement — the seventh day's closing.
29.35: The eighth day: a final intimate gathering — the closing assembly of Tabernacles. After the great week of abundance, one quieter day to linger in the LORD's presence.
29.36: The closing day's offering: one bull, one ram, seven lambs — reduced to intimacy after seven days of abundance.
29.37: Grain and drink in their prescribed proportions.
29.38: One final goat for atonement — sin addressed even on the last day.
29.39: This complete calendar — daily, Sabbath, new moon, Passover, Weeks, Trumpets, Atonement, Tabernacles — is Israel's minimum covenant commitment. Vows and freewill offerings come on top. Over Tabernacles' seven days, 70 bulls were offered in total — one for each nation in the ancient world, making Israel's sacrifice an intercession for all humanity.
29.40: Moses conveyed every detail to Israel exactly as the LORD had given it.

30.1: Moses addressed the tribal leaders directly with the LORD's instruction on vows.
30.2: A man who vows before the LORD — or swears an oath that binds him — is fully and unconditionally bound. His word is his obligation; he must perform every part of it.
30.3: A woman's vow, however, is subject to her household authority. If she is unmarried and living under her father when she vows —
30.4: — and her father hears it but says nothing, her vow holds in full. Silence is consent. Every pledge she made upon herself stands.
30.5: But if her father objects on the very day he hears it, not one of her vows holds. The LORD himself forgives her — the annulment is legitimate; no spiritual failure attaches to her.
30.6: If she is already married when she vows, or if a vow she made before marriage carries into her marriage —
30.7: and her husband hears it that day and says nothing — the vow stands. His silence ratifies it.
30.8: If he objects on the day he hears it, every vow is annulled — whatever she spoke, whatever she pledged. The LORD forgives her; the fault is not hers.
30.9: But widows and divorced women stand fully responsible for their own vows. No male authority can annul them; they hold entirely.
30.10: If she vowed while still in her husband's household —
30.11: and he heard it and said nothing — all her vows hold. Prolonged silence is affirmation.
30.12: But if he annuls them clearly on the day he hears — nothing stands. The LORD forgives her; what was undone by lawful authority was legitimately undone.
30.13: Any vow she makes — including fasts or acts of self-denial — her husband can confirm or cancel. The authority belongs to him in this household law.
30.14: But if he hears and waits day after day without speaking, he has confirmed every vow she carries. The critical moment is the day of hearing — silence then is binding.
30.15: If he later annuls what he had already ratified by silence, he takes her guilt onto himself. The broken vow's consequence falls on him.
30.16: These are the LORD's permanent regulations for vow-making within the household: husband and wife, father and unmarried daughter. The law acknowledges authority structures while protecting women from guilt when vows are legitimately annulled.
